Do you get your assignments done on time?” She believes it was “completely ad hoc”. After 37 gruelling minutes, having failed to find any wrongdoing, the Ice officers left the premises. To top it all off, Moitra Sarkar says, the Home Office vans left the restaurant car park without paying – non-customers are usually charged £2. The raid is one of more than 17,400 on businesses carried out since Labour came to power in July 2024 – a 77% increase on the year before and almost as many as in the entire previous parliament. The government says the drive has led to the highest number of arrests for illegal working since records began, but critics say the crackdown goes far beyond the party’s manifesto pledge of a “fair and properly managed immigration system”. While the UK’s Ice teams were set up in 2013 as part of Theresa May’s “hostile environment” policy, Labour has been using the increased number of raids to front much of the Home Office’s media output. In January, the Home Office set up a TikTok account, @SecureBordersUK, to show Ice officers raiding a market, a car wash and a nail bar. One video, which appears to show people struggling in open water during small-boat crossings, contains a stark message: “To the migrants who come to the UK illegally: you will face deportation or removal.” Most businesses raided by Ice don’t know why they were targeted. Anonymous tipoffs, which can be made online, appear to be behind many of the raids (including the one on Mandira’s Kitchen). Kevin Barker, a former Ice officer and the director of the paralegal firm Immigration Compliance Ltd, says that while raids are always “intelligence-led”, a tipoff can be enough to trigger one. There are steps between a tipoff being filed and a raid being carried out, Barker says. Surveillance is sometimes used, including “discreet drive-by surveillance”. Barker says Ice will also “see if there’s any other allegations or previous immigration raids to the business” and investigate if a name is given. But more often than not, he says, “there are no names mentioned – they just have a suspicion of illegal workers”. The nature of anonymity means a tipoff can relate to a personal or commercial grievance. Often, it’s “competitors within the local area” filling out the forms, he says. Moitra Sarkar holds a sponsor licence, which allows her to employ students or workers who might not normally have the right to work in the UK. “It was all very intimidating. Oh, you’re brown and you’re running a food business? Of course you’ve got illegal immigrants. It’s guilty until proven innocent,” she says. Racial profiling is a factor, says Seema Syeda, the advocacy and communications director at the Joint Council for the Welfare of Immigrants, which provides legal advice for those targeted by raids. She says the high number of arrests doesn’t paint the full picture. In 2025, Ice officers carried out 12,791 illegal-working raids and made 8,971 arrests. However, the Home Office’s figures show that only a quarter of those arrested (2,251) were detained and 12% (1,087) left the UK, either by force or voluntarily. Syeda says she has seen “many cases where people are raided, arrested and then released”. It is, she says, “very clear to us that this is a performative act”. *** When I arrive at Facing Heaven, the owner, Julian Denis, looks hesitant. I’m wearing all black and, for a moment, he worries Ice has returned. His business, a trendy vegan Chinese restaurant near Broadway Market in east London, was raided in September. It was Saturday night and the restaurant, decorated in bright pink and neon, was packed. About 10 officers made a beeline for the open-plan kitchen. They separated the staff before interrogating them individually, asking to see their IDs and payslips. Apart from speaking to the restaurant’s general manager, James Nolan, the officers didn’t speak to any white members of staff that night. They “singled out non-white, foreign-born workers for interrogation, scaring them with the possibility of being immediately jailed”, says Nolan. The lead officer told Nolan they were there off the back of an anonymous tipoff that specified “illegal Bengali workers”. After failing to find any illegal workers in the kitchen, having carried out their interrogations in plain sight of diners, the officers asked Nolan if there were any more members of staff on the premises. He said there weren’t. He was then asked the question again, replying with the same answer. Upon being asked a third time, Nolan got frustrated and said not “unless they’re on the fucking roof”. At this point, the officer “gave up trying to catch me out”, he says. The officer told Nolan that they were looking for “five to six illegal workers”. After about half an hour, with the dinner service halted, Ice left. Nolan asked an officer why they came during their busiest period, allowing customers to “look at this charade unfold”. The officer said they had to come at a “busy time when all staff were working”. Denis is from the US, where Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has instilled fear in communities of colour by rounding up and detaining people – many of them legal residents or even US citizens – using aggressive and violent tactics. This feeling has been intensified by the filmed killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti by federal agents. Denis describes the raid on his restaurant as “a wake-up call”. He says his employees, who are “legally employed and have the full right to work in the UK, should not have to come to work feeling fearful because of their country of origin”. Denis has a two-word message for the Home Office: “Fuck Ice.” Mandira’s Kitchen and Facing Heaven were raided under the Licensing Act 2003, which makes restaurants particularly vulnerable to surprise visits. It allows Ice officers to enter a business when it is carrying out a licensed activity, such as serving alcohol or selling hot food or drinks after 11pm, without a warrant. In 2025, 28% of all raids were carried out on restaurants, takeaways and cafes, a proportion significantly higher than for any other type of business. Barker claims that officers often go beyond the powers of the act, which allows them only to enter a business. Once inside, they treat it as though they have “carte blanche to speak to whoever they want to and search wherever they want to”. For example, Ice could enter a restaurant on the basis of concerns over someone without the right to work selling alcohol, but end up rounding up kitchen staff or delivery drivers, who “have no part in the sale of alcohol”. Ice was given greater power to enter businesses in 2017 after amendments to the Licensing Act were passed. The changes had some notable critics, including the MP for Holborn and St Pancras, Keir Starmer, who told the Commons in 2015 that they would give officers a “much lower threshold than the usual threshold for entering premises”. Barker says the amended act has proved “very powerful” for Ice: “Quite often, I would argue that their intentions have not been anything to do with the licensed activity. It’s just used to give them the power to enter.” In Surrey, Moitra Sarkar is still seeking answers. She sent freedom of information requests to the Home Office and Surrey police to find out why her business had been raided. The manager on shift at the time had overheard officers say they had received a tipoff, which has since been confirmed by documents she has obtained. These documents also reveal that the Home Office contacted the council to ask whether it had “licensing concerns” (it didn’t) and invited council officers to join the raid, which Moitra Sarkar believes was an attempt to beef up the legal justification. The “pre-visit report” lays out how Ice used the Licensing Act to authorise its raid without a warrant. It cites the cafe’s website, showing a menu including alcoholic drinks, as evidence that a licensed activity was taking place, which, combined with the tipoff, gave officers the power to enter. Officers are supposed to get “fully informed consent” from the business owner, which involves clearly saying why they are there and what they plan to do, as well as the potential civil or criminal penalties that could result from their visit. Businesses should also be told that they can refuse Ice officers entry and can withdraw their consent at any time. However, when Ice raided Mandira’s Kitchen, Moitra Sarkar says, the general manager asked the officer whether they could come back another day. The Surrey police officer told her: “No, that’s not how it works,” and pressed ahead anyway. When the Guardian asked about the raid, Surrey police said Ice and officers from its modern slavery and organised immigration crime unit carried out a joint visit “to complete a routine licensing check under section 179 of the Licensing Act 2003”. Fizza Qureshi, the CEO of the charity Migrants’ Rights Network (MRN), has spoken to a number of affected business owners, many of whom are from migrant backgrounds. She says many are “not fully informed of what their rights are” and have become resigned to raids being “part of their daily life”. The Home Office doesn’t record ethnicity data when carrying out raids or arrests (although it does record nationality), but research from MRN suggests they are carried out in areas that have large black and Asian populations. Qureshi has spoken to a car wash owner who says he is raided every three months or so, despite no illegal workers being found. The impact can be profound, she says, with raided businesses struggling to shake off the stigma. A Home Office spokesperson said: “All enforcement operations are intelligence-led. Race and ethnicity play no role in operational decisions. Illegal working undermines honest employers, undercuts local wages and fuels organised immigration crime.” “We hold our officers to the highest standards of professionalism and categorically refute any suggestion they acted improperly [or] were heavy‑handed,” the spokesperson added. Tanya Goldfarb, the head of business immigration at the law firm Bindman’s, says care homes, IT companies and hotels have also been targeted by Ice recently. Goldfarb says the raids “are frightening and intimidating” and that people may be handcuffed: “They are taken to detention centres. Sometimes they’re released and sometimes they’re not released. People often don’t know where their loved ones are. They don’t have a chance to make a phone call to anybody.” While Ice has far fewer powers than ICE in the US, this may not remain the case. Reform UK and the Conservatives have announced plans to create agencies modelled on ICE, should they win power. However, the increase in raids hasn’t come without opposition on the ground. Anti-raid groups are “springing up”, says Syeda. In January, a group in Lewisham, south London, alerted people to a car wash raid. When protesters told those being approached by Ice officers of their rights, no one was arrested. For Moitra Sarkar, the raid came during a particularly tough time. Days earlier, more than 100,000 people had marched through central London for “unite the kingdom”, a far-right rally at which racist conspiracy theories and anti-Muslim hate speech rang through Whitehall. Her daughter, who was born and raised in Surrey, was told not to go to work that week. “I’ve lived in Britain for 28 years and I’m the most optimistic person that there is,” says Moitra Sarkar. “But so many things happened that week.” Aside from the personal impact of the raid, Moitra Sarkar thinks it was a waste of officers’ time and taxpayers’ money. “We really need to call it out for what it is,” she says. “Completely ludicrous.” • Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in our letters section, please click here. • This article was amended on 24 March 2026. An earlier version referred to Ice officers being armed with stun guns during a raid; this was based on information from a witness account that was put to the Home Office prior to publication. However, the Home Office has since informed us that Ice officers do not possess such devices. An additional comment from the Home Office was added on 25 March 2026.

Title: ICE arrested an Oregon shop owner who had her green card in her pocket: ‘They didn’t care’
Date: 2026-03-06

The moment 19-year-old Emely Agustin spotted the group of masked immigration agents tackling a woman facedown on the pavement, her heart dropped. She saw the woman’s red jacket. “Oh my fucking God. That’s my mom!” she screamed. It was the morning of 5 November in Cottage Grove, in western Oregon, and masked officers with Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) had dragged Juanita Avila out of her van. The agents pinned her down as she cried out for her daughter, who started filming. Juanita, a 47-year-old legal permanent resident, had her green card in her pocket, but officers handcuffed and detained her. “I was really scared. I couldn’t stop shaking,” Emely recalled in a recent interview. “It was like they were trying to kidnap me,” added Juanita. The mother and daughter were speaking out for the first time since Juanita’s arrest became part of a court case that secured a major victory for immigrants’ rights in Oregon. The lawsuit challenged ICE’s tactic of detaining people without warrants or probable cause, a practice advocates say has fueled widespread racial profiling and chaotic arrests. Last month, a federal judge sided with the plaintiffs and issued a preliminary injunction blocking ICE agents in Oregon from continuing their “arrest first, justify later” tactics, as lawyers labeled it. The judge said ICE’s conduct had been “brutal and violent”, saying agents’ arrest strategy “systematically violates federal law and individual constitutional rights”. From ‘village girl’ to shop owner Standing behind the counter at Juanita’s Latina Store one recent morning, Juanita fidgeted with papers and spoke softly about her arrest as customers wandered in for snacks. Her tiny shop, located in a former bank vault, is on Main Street in Cottage Grove, a town south of Eugene. Juanita packs the shelves with an array of goods, including Latin American spices, corn masa flour, breads, cowboy hats and candles. Under a banner of Guatemalan flags, her window advertises money transfer services for immigrants to send funds to relatives. Juanita was born in 1978 in Guatemala. Her father was killed in war when she was three years old, and she immigrated to Los Angeles at age 14. In LA, she first lived with a family that exploited her and forced her to work, she said: “I was a village girl who was so innocent. I still have nightmares.” She later worked at a daycare near the University of California, Los Angeles, that served international families. The owner, an Iranian woman, helped take care of Juanita’s newborn son, and she grew to love Iranian festivities and food. Juanita moved to Cottage Grove with her three children in 2014. She felt safer and more at home in the “quiet and peace” of rural Oregon. She did farmwork, pruning blueberries and foraging for mushrooms. She was grateful for the fresh air, though the hours could be grueling. In 2016, the former owner of the local shop asked Juanita to take over the store. By then, she was known for helping local families with translations, with neighbors regularly asking her to interpret documents. “I didn’t want people coming into my house,” she says, with a laugh. “So this was perfect. I could have a business where I’d be helping the community.” Juanita, who got her green card in 2017, also began helping local police with interpretation. In Donald Trump’s first term, she started assisting neighbors facing deportation. One time, she recalled, she was kicked out of a federal building while trying to interpret for immigrants at their ICE check-ins: “They wanted any excuse for them to get deported. It was horrible.” She was haunted by the memory of an inconsolable three-year-old girl whose mother was detained. But as Trump returned to office, she continued to help immigrant neighbors, a practice that led her into ICE’s crosshairs. ‘I couldn’t breathe’ Around 5.30am on 5 November, Juanita awoke to a call from a friend, saying ICE had arrested a relative whose car was left behind, asking Juanita to retrieve the vehicle so it wouldn’t get towed. Juanita woke up her daughter, and the two drove in separate cars to search for the vehicle. They couldn’t find the car, and as Juanita was returning home, she spotted flashing lights behind her. She pulled over near a motel, assuming she was getting a traffic ticket from police. The men who approached drove unmarked cars, lacked visible badges, asked where she was born and said she was under arrest, she later wrote in a declaration filed as part of the lawsuit. When she responded asking why she was stopped, an officer did not show a warrant or give a reason, her declaration said. The officers reached through her window, opened the door, pulled her out and kneeled on her back, the declaration said. They also accused her of being “illegal”, she wrote. One witness video captured Juanita facedown yelling through sobs: “Why are you arresting me? You are not police … Emely! Emely!” Emely, her US citizen daughter, soon arrived. Footage Emely recorded of the incident shows she immediately explained that her mom had a green card and was carrying it on her. When she yelled to her mom that she was recording, an officer shined a bright light in her face, obscuring her camera’s view. “You don’t need to tackle her. There’s no reason to fucking do this,” Emely pleaded. The officers handcuffed Juanita and put her in their car. One officer then told Emely she could also be arrested for “obstruction”, witness video shows. The officers eventually uncuffed Juanita so she could grab her green card. When she again asked why they had arrested her and didn’t believe her, an agent responded: “Because they lie all the time,” according to Emely’s footage. Juanita was released. The encounter did not lead to charges and lasted roughly 30 minutes. One of the scariest moments, Juanita recalled, was being thrown into ICE’s vehicle “like a log”, leaving her weeping and coughing through a panic attack: “I couldn’t breathe.” While under arrest, she thought of her 12-year-old son with autism, fearing what would happen to him if she were jailed: “I said: ‘My son is at home. Don’t take me. Don’t do this.’ They didn’t listen. They didn’t care.” Juanita said she was terrified for her daughter, but added: “If Emely wasn’t there, they would have taken me.” Emely, who is studying to teach English as a second language, said after the agent shined the light in her face, she fell off a curb and badly injured her knee: “My voice was trembling and I thought if I was arrested, who is going to help my mom? … All I wanted to do was hug her.” Juanita feared agents had broken her shoulder and went to the hospital. She then visited the police station to report the incident. Forced into hiding Juanita’s arrest stunned Cottage Grove residents. “A lot of people did not believe ICE was doing these things until they saw what happened to Juanita,” said Abelio Carrillo Chales, an organizer with the Rural Organizing Project, a non-profit headquartered in town. “People were shocked and horrified, because she is well-known and has been here for so long. People were really concerned about the safety of their neighbors.” Locals organized a vigil to support Juanita and others affected by ICE and began doing early morning patrols and joining rapid response networks, Carrillo said. DHS later confirmed ICE had detained nine people in Cottage Grove that day. Three of those arrested were Carrillo’s family members, he said. One has since been deported to Guatemala; another was released and a third remains detained. The November operation, Carrillo said, sent many immigrants into hiding, regardless of their status. Children stayed home from school. Parents skipped work. Families struggled to keep up with necessities, like groceries. Carrillo, who was on the scene for Juanita’s arrest, said he was shaken by the episode and had to take time off work. He felt anguish over his inability to intervene, but knew if he got any closer, he risked detention: “I was really in fear for my life.” Emely said she had to go on medications for depression and PTSD and found herself shaking anytime a suspicious car drove by. As Juanita’s bruises faded, she became overwhelmed by the community support. She had spent her whole life helping others and didn’t know how to respond to residents leaving notes and flowers at the store and offering assistance, she said. For more than a month, her older son ran the shop while she largely stayed in bed. “My therapist said, you better get up. Go back to work. Show them you’re not defeated. The community needs you,” she said. Juanita soon was helping others affected by ICE’s crackdown, bringing food to a young girl who had stopped eating after her father was detained and picking up farmworkers who had been hiding from ICE in the woods. And she joined the lawsuit. “I was scared, because it’s the government. Who is going to win against the government?” she said. It was painful to keep retelling her account, she added: “But I told Emely, we have to tell our story. Our voices have to be heard.” ICE quotas and an app In January, Juanita’s arrest became part of a class-action suit filed by the Innovation Law Lab, an Oregon-based immigrants’ rights non-profit. Without warrants, the attorneys said, ICE can only arrest people if there is probable cause that the person is in the country without authorization and “likely to escape before a warrant can be obtained”. Instead, ICE was consistently violating people’s due-process rights, often stopping them based on their skin color, with no justification. The suit exposed details of how ICE had targeted people without warrants, with officers testifying about the Department of Homeland Security’s “Operation Black Rose”, which launched in Portland last fall and, according to DHS, led to more than 1,200 arrests through mid-December. One ICE officer testified that Black Rose teams of roughly nine to 12 officers were told to target eight arrests per day, with plaintiff’s attorneys estimating this amounted to a daily quota of 50 arrests across Oregon. The officer also revealed agents chose locations with help from a DHS app called “Elite”, which identified target areas where there might be multiple people with some “nexus” to immigration (including documented immigrants without violations). “It’s a dragnet of arresting whoever, wherever just because they look a certain way or work in a certain industry or don’t speak English,” said Nelly Garcia Orjuela, Innovation Law Lab staff attorney. Victor Cruz Gamez, the leading named plaintiff, has lived in the US for 25 years and has a work permit and protection against deportation. The father of three was nonetheless arrested by ICE in October when agents mixed him up with a different Victor Cruz, and he spent three weeks in detention before he was released. In an interview, he said he wanted to protect others and show that the government was not targeting “criminals and rapists”, as DHS has claimed. “They just see us as numbers. They don’t see us as human beings,” he said. ‘There is hope’ On 4 February, the US judge Mustafa Kasubhai sided with Innovation Law Lab, broadly halting ICE’s warrantless arrests in Oregon, saying agents could only detain people without warrants if the agents produced individualized narratives documenting the probable cause they had pre-arrest to carry out the detention. “There can be no injury more irreparable than being illegally arrested in the manner in which [plaintiffs were] stopped, detained, thrown to the ground and handcuffed,” said the judge, who said Juanita’s arrest was “without any justification”. Oregon is only the third jurisdiction with these protections, which federal judges ordered in similar cases in Colorado and Washington DC. Cruz said the ruling meant people could feel comfortable leaving their homes for the first time to get groceries or go to the doctor: “I am so relieved … There is still hope.” It’s unclear whether officers have complied with the ruling, though attorneys said there were anecdotal reports of reduced ICE activity. In Colorado, ICE has been accused of violating the federal order blocking warrantless arrests. DHS and the US justice department did not respond to inquiries about the ruling or Juanita’s arrest. Thomas Ratcliffe, the US attorney, had argued Juanita’s arrest should be excluded from the case since it was an “investigative detention”, not an arrest, but the judge rejected that argument. Ratcliffe in court also apologized to Cruz for what he had endured and expressed remorse to Emely after her testimony. He acknowledged her mother’s arrest had interfered with her final exams, saying: “I hope that you, your brother and your whole family can put this behind you.” For her part, Emely said the ruling offered some solace, though she questioned whether officers would follow it, saying: “It’s the only way my family is going to get justice.” Her family, she said, was slowly moving forward, but she has noticed changes in her mother, who was once outgoing, but is now reserved. Her mom often sits in her store, watching in fear as vehicles pass by. Emely, who recently turned 20, also said her 12-year-old brother became afraid to go to school, began lashing out and started obsessively watching ICE videos, which caused further distress. He is now in therapy, she said, and she has made sure that he never saw the footage she took of their mom’s arrest.

conversation.rag_input: How did the officers' attempts to assert their authority by questioning the legitimacy of the individuals present lead to different outcomes for those interactions?
conversation.question: How did the immigration officers' attempts to assert their authority by questioning the legitimacy of the individuals present in the restaurant and the shop influence the immediate progression of those encounters?
conversation.answer: In the restaurant raid, the officers' attempt to assert authority by questioning the existence of illegal workers led the owner to reach a breaking point of frustration where he told them to stop trying to 'catch him out', resulting in the officers eventually giving up and leaving. In the shop owner's arrest, the officers' assertion of authority by accusing the shop owner of being 'illegal' and disregarding her green card led to an escalation where the owner was physically tackled and her daughter was threatened with arrest for obstruction to suppress her advocacy.
conversation.rag_answer:
The officers' attempts to assert their authority by questioning the legitimacy of individuals led to different outcomes in the UK restaurant raids compared to the US shop owner's arrest.

In the UK restaurant raids on Mandira's Kitchen and Facing Heaven, immigration officers asserted authority by singling out "non-white, foreign-born workers for interrogation," asking detailed personal questions about names, family, studies, and academic performance, and demanding IDs and payslips. The owner of Mandira's Kitchen noted the questions were "completely ad hoc" and believed racial profiling was involved, with officers operating under an "assumption of guilt." Officers did not explain their presence or ask for permission. This questioning of legitimacy led to staff being subjected to intrusive, individually targeted interrogations on their business premises, creating an atmosphere of suspicion and harassment based on perceived illegitimacy.

Conversely, during the arrest of shop owner Juanita Avila in Oregon by US ICE, the assertion of authority and questioning of legitimacy was immediate and physically aggressive. Officers pulled her from her van, pinned her down, and handcuffed her, accusing her of being "illegal" without a warrant or reason. When her daughter intervened, an officer used a bright light to obscure recording. Even after Juanita presented her green card, an agent dismissed it, stating, "Because they lie all the time," demonstrating a pre-existing disbelief in her legitimacy. In this case, the officers' assertion of authority and questioning of legitimacy, despite immediate proof to the contrary, led to her being violently arrested, thrown into an ICE vehicle "like a log," and suffering a panic attack, despite being a legal resident.

conversation.rag_input: Does the way officers dismissed the pushback in both these cases suggest a consistent strategy for handling resistance to their enforcement actions?
conversation.question: What common strategic approach did immigration officers demonstrate when dismissing the pushback encountered during both the restaurant raid and the shop owner's arrest?
conversation.answer: In both encounters, the officers adopted a strategy of persistent assertion of control to override resistance. When faced with the restaurant owner’s frustration, the officer used repetitive interrogation to try to force a contradiction, whereas, when faced with the daughter's advocacy and documentation, the officers used physical intimidation, such as shining lights in her face and threatening her with arrest, to suppress the challenge and maintain dominance over the situation.
conversation.rag_answer:
The way officers dismissed pushback in the UK restaurant raids and the US shop owner's arrest suggests different, rather than consistent, strategies for handling resistance to their enforcement actions.

In the UK restaurant raids, the dismissal of potential pushback was more subtle and procedural. Officers did not explain their presence or ask for permission to question staff, effectively dismissing any implicit resistance or questioning of their authority by simply proceeding with their actions. The owner's observation of "ad hoc" questions and belief in racial profiling, while a form of pushback from the business owner, was seemingly ignored as officers continued their interrogations under an "assumption of guilt."

Conversely, during the US shop owner's arrest, the dismissal of pushback was immediate, direct, and aggressive. When Juanita Avila's daughter intervened and stated Juanita had a green card, an officer actively obstructed her by shining a bright light in her face to obscure her recording. Furthermore, when Juanita presented her green card, an agent verbally dismissed this direct evidence of legitimacy by stating, "Because they lie all the time," demonstrating a pre-existing disbelief and outright rejection of the presented proof. This indicates a strategy of actively discrediting and physically impeding resistance.

Therefore, while both scenarios involved officers asserting authority and dismissing challenges, the methods employed were distinct: the UK officers dismissed challenges by ignoring them and proceeding with their established procedures, while the US officers actively countered and discredited direct resistance through physical obstruction and verbal dismissal of evidence.

conversation.rag_input: Is it possible to tell if the officers' reactions were based more on the individuals' specific actions or on the context of where the enforcement was happening?
conversation.question: Does the response of immigration officers to pushback in the restaurant raid and the shop owner's arrest suggest that their behavior was primarily driven by the specific actions taken by those they encountered or by the environmental context of the raid and arrest?
conversation.answer: The officers' responses suggest they reacted to both factors rather than one alone. In the restaurant, the environmental context of a 'busy time' was used by the officer to justify the timing of the raid, and the manager's verbal pushback eventually caused the officer to stop his specific line of questioning. In the shop arrest, the environmental context of a public road stop facilitated an immediate physical intervention, while the specific action of the daughter filming prompted an immediate reactive shift by officers to shine a light in her face and threaten her with arrest. Thus, officers appear to adapt their tactics based on both the environmental setting and the specific nature of the resistance encountered.
conversation.rag_answer:
It is possible to discern that both the context of where the enforcement was happening and the officers' perceptions of the individuals present significantly influenced their reactions, though in different ways across the two cases.

In the **UK restaurant raids** on Mandira's Kitchen and Facing Heaven, the *context of the business premises* heavily dictated the *type* of enforcement action. The officers conducted on-site interrogations, separating staff and asking detailed personal questions within the workplace. Within this context, their reactions were largely based on their *preconceived notions about the individuals' identities and legitimacy*. Officers "singled out non-white, foreign-born workers for interrogation," operating under an "assumption of guilt" and believing racial profiling was a factor. Their questions were "ad hoc," suggesting a lack of specific evidence but a strong focus on the perceived 'otherness' of the individuals. The non-explanation of their presence also reflects a strategy suited to an internal business environment.

Conversely, during the **US shop owner's arrest** in Oregon, the *public setting* (pulling Juanita Avila from her van) led to an immediate, physically aggressive arrest. The officers' initial accusations of her being "illegal" and their physical actions (pinning her down, handcuffing her, throwing her into the vehicle) were based on a *pre-judgment of her legitimacy*, irrespective of her actual legal status. Her specific action of presenting a green card was met with a dismissal ("Because they lie all the time"), indicating a deep-seated disbelief in the individual's truthfulness. Similarly, her daughter's specific action of attempting to record was met with a direct obstructive reaction (shining a light).

Therefore, while the *location and setting* (workplace vs. public street) strongly influenced the *mode* of enforcement (interrogation vs. immediate physical arrest), the officers' *reactions and specific tactics* within those modes were consistently driven by their pre-existing assumptions about the *individuals' perceived illegitimacy*, often overriding any specific actions or evidence provided by those individuals. It's a complex interplay where context set the stage, but the officers' internal biases about the individuals drove the specific unfolding of the interactions.

conversation.rag_input: Does that mean the officers are more likely to escalate the situation when they feel their control is being challenged by a third party, compared to when they are just dealing with the person they are targeting?
conversation.question: When comparing the restaurant raid and the shop owner's arrest, is there evidence that immigration officers are more prone to escalating their behavior when a third party challenges their control than when the primary target of the enforcement challenges them?
conversation.answer: The evidence shows that when the primary target challenged the officers in the restaurant, the officer eventually backed down from his line of questioning after the owner's persistent verbal pushback. Conversely, in the shop arrest, the involvement of a third party—the daughter—prompted the officers to escalate their tactics by using a bright light to obstruct her and threatening her with arrest for 'obstruction.' This suggests that officers are indeed more likely to use escalating or defensive countermeasures when their authority is challenged by an external observer than when they are faced with verbal pushback from the primary individual being targeted.
conversation.rag_answer:
Based on the provided information, the US case involving Juanita Avila's arrest suggests that officers were more prone to immediate, active escalation when their control was challenged by a third party, compared to how they handled challenges from the targeted individual.

During Juanita Avila's arrest, when the **targeted individual** (Juanita) directly challenged the officers' assertion of her illegitimacy by presenting her green card, the officer dismissed it verbally, stating, "Because they lie all the time." While this was a dismissal of evidence, it was not an *escalation* of physical force or obstruction beyond the already aggressive arrest that had taken place. The physical actions (pinning her down, handcuffing, throwing her into the vehicle) had already occurred before she presented the card.

Conversely, when a **third party** (Juanita's daughter) intervened by stating Juanita had a green card and attempting to record the interaction, an officer directly and actively escalated by shining a bright light in her daughter's face to obscure her recording. This was a specific, obstructive action taken in direct response to the third party's challenge to their control and attempt to document the situation.

The UK restaurant raids do not offer a clear parallel for escalation in response to direct challenges, whether from targeted individuals or third parties. In those instances, officers primarily dismissed implicit pushback by ignoring it and continuing with their established procedures, rather than escalating their tactics.

conversation.rag_input: How do the interrogation tactics used by immigration officers in March 2026 compare across the restaurant raid and the shop owner's arrest?
conversation.question: How do the interrogation tactics used by immigration officers in March 2026 compare across the restaurant raid and the shop owner's arrest?
conversation.answer: In the March 24, 2026, restaurant raid, officers used repeated questioning to try to catch the owner out and justify their presence based on an anonymous tipoff about 'illegal Bengali workers' during a busy service. In the March 6, 2026, shop owner incident, officers used physical force, including kneeling on the subject's back and handcuffing her without showing a warrant, while actively obscuring documentation efforts by the owner's daughter and threatening her with arrest for 'obstruction'.
conversation.rag_answer:
Based on the context provided, both the restaurant raids in the UK and the shop owner's arrest in the US, reported in March 2026, illustrate aggressive and often legally questionable interrogation tactics by immigration officers, though with distinct differences in their intensity and specific methods.

Here's a comparison of the interrogation tactics:

**Restaurant Raids (UK - Mandira's Kitchen & Facing Heaven, September, reported March 2026):**

*   **Surprise and Intimidation:** Officers (11-strong unit for Mandira's Kitchen, ~10 for Facing Heaven), clad in stab vests, blocked entrances, and stormed the premises without warning or warrant (under the Licensing Act 2003). They chose busy times to ensure all staff were present and customers witnessed the "charade."
*   **Lack of Explanation/Consent:** Officers did not explain their presence or ask for permission. Mandira Moitra Sarkar states they "didn’t explain. They didn’t ask for permission." When her general manager asked if they could come back another day, a Surrey police officer stated, "No, that’s not how it works." Officers did not obtain "fully informed consent."
*   **Separation and Individual Questioning:** Staff members were separated and questioned individually, often in plain sight of diners.
*   **Targeted Questioning and Racial Profiling:** Officers "singled out non-white, foreign-born workers for interrogation." At Facing Heaven, they explicitly looked for "illegal Bengali workers" based on an anonymous tipoff and did not question white staff members. Mandira Moitra Sarkar felt it was "completely ad hoc" and racially motivated ("Oh, you’re brown and you’re running a food business? Of course you’ve got illegal immigrants. It’s guilty until proven innocent").
*   **Probing Personal Details:** Questions went beyond work eligibility, delving into personal lives. Examples include: "When was the last time you went to university? What grades do you get? Do you get your assignments done on time?" One employee was quizzed on his wife and child, another on his studies after showing a university ID. Officers also insisted on a specific name for an employee (Shamriaz vs. Shabbir).
*   **Repeated Questioning to "Catch Out":** At Facing Heaven, officers repeatedly asked if there were more staff on the premises even after being told no, seemingly trying to "catch out" the manager.
*   **Exceeding Legal Powers:** Critics like Kevin Barker argue officers often go beyond the powers of the Licensing Act, treating entry as "carte blanche to speak to whoever they want to and search wherever they want to," even if the staff in question have no role in the licensed activity cited for entry.

**Shop Owner's Arrest (US - Juanita Avila, Oregon, November, reported March 2026):**

*   **Roadside Stop and Immediate Physical Force:** Juanita Avila was pulled over by officers in unmarked cars, who immediately reached through her window, opened the door, pulled her out, and kneeled on her back, pinning her facedown on the pavement.
*   **Lack of Warrant/Reason for Arrest:** Officers did not show a warrant or give a reason for the stop or arrest. They stated she was under arrest without explanation.
*   **Ignoring Evidence of Legal Status:** Despite Juanita being a legal permanent resident with her green card in her pocket, officers handcuffed and detained her, accusing her of being "illegal." When her daughter Emely stated her mom had a green card, an agent later responded: "Because they lie all the time."
*   **Intimidation of Witnesses/Family:** An officer shined a bright light in Emely's face while she was filming, obscuring her camera's view. Emely was also threatened with arrest for "obstruction."
*   **Brutal and Violent Treatment:** Juanita was thrown into the ICE vehicle "like a log," causing her to weep and cough through a panic attack. Her conduct was later described by a federal judge as "brutal and violent."
*   **Disregard for Personal Circumstances:** Officers ignored Juanita's pleas about her 12-year-old son with autism.
*   **"Arrest First, Justify Later" Tactic:** This was a core finding of the subsequent lawsuit, where officers detained people without warrants or probable cause, often based on appearance or industry, as part of "Operation Black Rose" which had daily arrest quotas.

**Comparison:**

*   **Similarities:**
    *   **Lack of Transparency:** In both cases, officers failed to provide clear explanations for their actions or obtain consent from those being questioned/arrested.
    *   **Assumption of Guilt:** Officers in both scenarios operated under an immediate assumption of illegal status or wrongdoing, placing the burden of proof on the individuals.
    *   **Targeting:** While the UK raids explicitly "singled out non-white, foreign-born workers," the US arrest of Juanita (and the broader "Operation Black Rose") was criticized for stopping people based on skin color and for targeting areas with "nexus" to immigration, regardless of documented status.
    *   **Intimidation and Fear:** Both sets of tactics were highly intimidating, causing significant distress, fear, and even physical injury (Emely's knee, Juanita's panic attack, feared broken shoulder).
    *   **Questionable Legality:** In the UK, officers were accused of exceeding the powers of the Licensing Act. In the US, a federal judge ruled that ICE's "arrest first, justify later" tactics systematically violated federal law and constitutional rights.

*   **Differences:**
    *   **Physicality:** Juanita Avila's arrest involved immediate and significant physical force (tackling, pinning, handcuffing, throwing into vehicle), which was not described in the restaurant raids. The UK raids were intimidating but less physically violent towards the staff during the interrogation itself.
    *   **Location of Encounter:** One was a roadside arrest; the others were raids on established business premises.
    *   **Interrogation Focus:** While both were broad, the UK restaurant raids involved more detailed, probing questions about personal history (university, family) seemingly designed to find inconsistencies or establish a lack of right to work. Juanita's arrest was characterized by immediate detention and accusations of being "illegal," with less detailed questioning *during* the initial encounter, and more about dismissing her legal status.
    *   **Legal Outcome:** Juanita's case directly led to a federal injunction against ICE's warrantless arrest tactics in Oregon, explicitly blocking them. While the UK raids were criticized for exceeding powers, a similar broad legal ruling against the Home Office's general tactics described in the article has not yet occurred, though Mandira Moitra Sarkar is seeking answers.

In summary, while both contexts reveal a pattern of aggressive, intimidating, and often legally dubious immigration enforcement tactics reported in March 2026, the US incident involving Juanita Avila was marked by a more immediate and severe application of physical force and a blatant disregard for existing legal documentation, leading to a significant legal victory for immigrants' rights in Oregon. The UK restaurant raids focused more on psychological intimidation, intrusive questioning of personal details, and the misuse of existing licensing laws to gain entry and conduct broad interrogations.

Title: ‘They singled out non-white, foreign-born workers’: the restaurants raided by Britain’s version of ICE 
Date: 2026-03-24

Diners were tucking into their upmarket Indian lunch when the Ice agents slid through the restaurant’s back fence. Clad in stab vests, the 11-strong unit blocked off every entrance before moving in on their target: Mandira’s Kitchen. This wasn’t a scene from California or Texas. It happened near Guildford, England, among the rolling Surrey Hills. Before the Home Office’s immigration compliance and enforcement (Ice) officers stormed the restaurant in September, they came up with a codeword in the event they were attacked with any weapons that might be at hand in a kitchen. What they found were customers eating biryani and samosas in a converted barn decorated with plants and a rickshaw bicycle hanging from the ceiling. When they reached the kitchen, they found five junior members of staff cooking. The officers demanded to see their passports. “They didn’t explain. They didn’t ask for permission,” says the restaurant’s owner, Mandira Moitra Sarkar. That 11 officers could burst into her business with no warrant and question staff is “astounding”, she says. Moitra Sarkar was on holiday in Tanzania when Ice arrived; she was notified by a frantic call from a member of staff. Moitra Sarkar made her name in the restaurant industry after being lauded by culinary giants including Nigella Lawson and Jamie Oliver. Her prepared dishes, which can be bought online for home delivery, were featured on a Channel 4 competition to find the country’s best ready meals for Aldi. But this wasn’t enough for the Home Office. Its crackdown on illegal working has left many business owners and employees reeling from its heavy-handed tactics. The immigration officers – from South Central Ice, which covers a patchwork of southern England including Portsmouth, Plymouth and Surrey – were accompanied by two officers from Surrey police, who walked around the restaurant among the customers, watching the scene unfold, and then blocked entry to the toilets. Once the Ice officers entered the kitchen, the members of staff were separated and questioned individually. One employee was asked whether his name was really Shamriaz (officers insisted it was Shabbir); another was quizzed on his wife and his child; one was asked about their studies after showing a university ID. Moitra Sarkar says the officers asked questions such as: “When was the last time you went to university? What grades do you get? Do you get your assignments done on time?” She believes it was “completely ad hoc”. After 37 gruelling minutes, having failed to find any wrongdoing, the Ice officers left the premises. To top it all off, Moitra Sarkar says, the Home Office vans left the restaurant car park without paying – non-customers are usually charged £2. The raid is one of more than 17,400 on businesses carried out since Labour came to power in July 2024 – a 77% increase on the year before and almost as many as in the entire previous parliament. The government says the drive has led to the highest number of arrests for illegal working since records began, but critics say the crackdown goes far beyond the party’s manifesto pledge of a “fair and properly managed immigration system”. While the UK’s Ice teams were set up in 2013 as part of Theresa May’s “hostile environment” policy, Labour has been using the increased number of raids to front much of the Home Office’s media output. In January, the Home Office set up a TikTok account, @SecureBordersUK, to show Ice officers raiding a market, a car wash and a nail bar. One video, which appears to show people struggling in open water during small-boat crossings, contains a stark message: “To the migrants who come to the UK illegally: you will face deportation or removal.” Most businesses raided by Ice don’t know why they were targeted. Anonymous tipoffs, which can be made online, appear to be behind many of the raids (including the one on Mandira’s Kitchen). Kevin Barker, a former Ice officer and the director of the paralegal firm Immigration Compliance Ltd, says that while raids are always “intelligence-led”, a tipoff can be enough to trigger one. There are steps between a tipoff being filed and a raid being carried out, Barker says. Surveillance is sometimes used, including “discreet drive-by surveillance”. Barker says Ice will also “see if there’s any other allegations or previous immigration raids to the business” and investigate if a name is given. But more often than not, he says, “there are no names mentioned – they just have a suspicion of illegal workers”. The nature of anonymity means a tipoff can relate to a personal or commercial grievance. Often, it’s “competitors within the local area” filling out the forms, he says. Moitra Sarkar holds a sponsor licence, which allows her to employ students or workers who might not normally have the right to work in the UK. “It was all very intimidating. Oh, you’re brown and you’re running a food business? Of course you’ve got illegal immigrants. It’s guilty until proven innocent,” she says. Racial profiling is a factor, says Seema Syeda, the advocacy and communications director at the Joint Council for the Welfare of Immigrants, which provides legal advice for those targeted by raids. She says the high number of arrests doesn’t paint the full picture. In 2025, Ice officers carried out 12,791 illegal-working raids and made 8,971 arrests. However, the Home Office’s figures show that only a quarter of those arrested (2,251) were detained and 12% (1,087) left the UK, either by force or voluntarily. Syeda says she has seen “many cases where people are raided, arrested and then released”. It is, she says, “very clear to us that this is a performative act”. *** When I arrive at Facing Heaven, the owner, Julian Denis, looks hesitant. I’m wearing all black and, for a moment, he worries Ice has returned. His business, a trendy vegan Chinese restaurant near Broadway Market in east London, was raided in September. It was Saturday night and the restaurant, decorated in bright pink and neon, was packed. About 10 officers made a beeline for the open-plan kitchen. They separated the staff before interrogating them individually, asking to see their IDs and payslips. Apart from speaking to the restaurant’s general manager, James Nolan, the officers didn’t speak to any white members of staff that night. They “singled out non-white, foreign-born workers for interrogation, scaring them with the possibility of being immediately jailed”, says Nolan. The lead officer told Nolan they were there off the back of an anonymous tipoff that specified “illegal Bengali workers”. After failing to find any illegal workers in the kitchen, having carried out their interrogations in plain sight of diners, the officers asked Nolan if there were any more members of staff on the premises. He said there weren’t. He was then asked the question again, replying with the same answer. Upon being asked a third time, Nolan got frustrated and said not “unless they’re on the fucking roof”. At this point, the officer “gave up trying to catch me out”, he says. The officer told Nolan that they were looking for “five to six illegal workers”. After about half an hour, with the dinner service halted, Ice left. Nolan asked an officer why they came during their busiest period, allowing customers to “look at this charade unfold”. The officer said they had to come at a “busy time when all staff were working”. Denis is from the US, where Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has instilled fear in communities of colour by rounding up and detaining people – many of them legal residents or even US citizens – using aggressive and violent tactics. This feeling has been intensified by the filmed killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti by federal agents. Denis describes the raid on his restaurant as “a wake-up call”. He says his employees, who are “legally employed and have the full right to work in the UK, should not have to come to work feeling fearful because of their country of origin”. Denis has a two-word message for the Home Office: “Fuck Ice.” Mandira’s Kitchen and Facing Heaven were raided under the Licensing Act 2003, which makes restaurants particularly vulnerable to surprise visits. It allows Ice officers to enter a business when it is carrying out a licensed activity, such as serving alcohol or selling hot food or drinks after 11pm, without a warrant. In 2025, 28% of all raids were carried out on restaurants, takeaways and cafes, a proportion significantly higher than for any other type of business. Barker claims that officers often go beyond the powers of the act, which allows them only to enter a business. Once inside, they treat it as though they have “carte blanche to speak to whoever they want to and search wherever they want to”. For example, Ice could enter a restaurant on the basis of concerns over someone without the right to work selling alcohol, but end up rounding up kitchen staff or delivery drivers, who “have no part in the sale of alcohol”. Ice was given greater power to enter businesses in 2017 after amendments to the Licensing Act were passed. The changes had some notable critics, including the MP for Holborn and St Pancras, Keir Starmer, who told the Commons in 2015 that they would give officers a “much lower threshold than the usual threshold for entering premises”. Barker says the amended act has proved “very powerful” for Ice: “Quite often, I would argue that their intentions have not been anything to do with the licensed activity. It’s just used to give them the power to enter.” In Surrey, Moitra Sarkar is still seeking answers. She sent freedom of information requests to the Home Office and Surrey police to find out why her business had been raided. The manager on shift at the time had overheard officers say they had received a tipoff, which has since been confirmed by documents she has obtained. These documents also reveal that the Home Office contacted the council to ask whether it had “licensing concerns” (it didn’t) and invited council officers to join the raid, which Moitra Sarkar believes was an attempt to beef up the legal justification. The “pre-visit report” lays out how Ice used the Licensing Act to authorise its raid without a warrant. It cites the cafe’s website, showing a menu including alcoholic drinks, as evidence that a licensed activity was taking place, which, combined with the tipoff, gave officers the power to enter. Officers are supposed to get “fully informed consent” from the business owner, which involves clearly saying why they are there and what they plan to do, as well as the potential civil or criminal penalties that could result from their visit. Businesses should also be told that they can refuse Ice officers entry and can withdraw their consent at any time. However, when Ice raided Mandira’s Kitchen, Moitra Sarkar says, the general manager asked the officer whether they could come back another day. The Surrey police officer told her: “No, that’s not how it works,” and pressed ahead anyway. When the Guardian asked about the raid, Surrey police said Ice and officers from its modern slavery and organised immigration crime unit carried out a joint visit “to complete a routine licensing check under section 179 of the Licensing Act 2003”. Fizza Qureshi, the CEO of the charity Migrants’ Rights Network (MRN), has spoken to a number of affected business owners, many of whom are from migrant backgrounds. She says many are “not fully informed of what their rights are” and have become resigned to raids being “part of their daily life”. The Home Office doesn’t record ethnicity data when carrying out raids or arrests (although it does record nationality), but research from MRN suggests they are carried out in areas that have large black and Asian populations. Qureshi has spoken to a car wash owner who says he is raided every three months or so, despite no illegal workers being found. The impact can be profound, she says, with raided businesses struggling to shake off the stigma. A Home Office spokesperson said: “All enforcement operations are intelligence-led. Race and ethnicity play no role in operational decisions. Illegal working undermines honest employers, undercuts local wages and fuels organised immigration crime.” “We hold our officers to the highest standards of professionalism and categorically refute any suggestion they acted improperly [or] were heavy‑handed,” the spokesperson added. Tanya Goldfarb, the head of business immigration at the law firm Bindman’s, says care homes, IT companies and hotels have also been targeted by Ice recently. Goldfarb says the raids “are frightening and intimidating” and that people may be handcuffed: “They are taken to detention centres. Sometimes they’re released and sometimes they’re not released. People often don’t know where their loved ones are. They don’t have a chance to make a phone call to anybody.” While Ice has far fewer powers than ICE in the US, this may not remain the case. Reform UK and the Conservatives have announced plans to create agencies modelled on ICE, should they win power. However, the increase in raids hasn’t come without opposition on the ground. Anti-raid groups are “springing up”, says Syeda. In January, a group in Lewisham, south London, alerted people to a car wash raid. When protesters told those being approached by Ice officers of their rights, no one was arrested. For Moitra Sarkar, the raid came during a particularly tough time. Days earlier, more than 100,000 people had marched through central London for “unite the kingdom”, a far-right rally at which racist conspiracy theories and anti-Muslim hate speech rang through Whitehall. Her daughter, who was born and raised in Surrey, was told not to go to work that week. “I’ve lived in Britain for 28 years and I’m the most optimistic person that there is,” says Moitra Sarkar. “But so many things happened that week.” Aside from the personal impact of the raid, Moitra Sarkar thinks it was a waste of officers’ time and taxpayers’ money. “We really need to call it out for what it is,” she says. “Completely ludicrous.” • Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in our letters section, please click here. • This article was amended on 24 March 2026. An earlier version referred to Ice officers being armed with stun guns during a raid; this was based on information from a witness account that was put to the Home Office prior to publication. However, the Home Office has since informed us that Ice officers do not possess such devices. An additional comment from the Home Office was added on 25 March 2026.

Title: ICE arrested an Oregon shop owner who had her green card in her pocket: ‘They didn’t care’
Date: 2026-03-06

The moment 19-year-old Emely Agustin spotted the group of masked immigration agents tackling a woman facedown on the pavement, her heart dropped. She saw the woman’s red jacket. “Oh my fucking God. That’s my mom!” she screamed. It was the morning of 5 November in Cottage Grove, in western Oregon, and masked officers with Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) had dragged Juanita Avila out of her van. The agents pinned her down as she cried out for her daughter, who started filming. Juanita, a 47-year-old legal permanent resident, had her green card in her pocket, but officers handcuffed and detained her. “I was really scared. I couldn’t stop shaking,” Emely recalled in a recent interview. “It was like they were trying to kidnap me,” added Juanita. The mother and daughter were speaking out for the first time since Juanita’s arrest became part of a court case that secured a major victory for immigrants’ rights in Oregon. The lawsuit challenged ICE’s tactic of detaining people without warrants or probable cause, a practice advocates say has fueled widespread racial profiling and chaotic arrests. Last month, a federal judge sided with the plaintiffs and issued a preliminary injunction blocking ICE agents in Oregon from continuing their “arrest first, justify later” tactics, as lawyers labeled it. The judge said ICE’s conduct had been “brutal and violent”, saying agents’ arrest strategy “systematically violates federal law and individual constitutional rights”. From ‘village girl’ to shop owner Standing behind the counter at Juanita’s Latina Store one recent morning, Juanita fidgeted with papers and spoke softly about her arrest as customers wandered in for snacks. Her tiny shop, located in a former bank vault, is on Main Street in Cottage Grove, a town south of Eugene. Juanita packs the shelves with an array of goods, including Latin American spices, corn masa flour, breads, cowboy hats and candles. Under a banner of Guatemalan flags, her window advertises money transfer services for immigrants to send funds to relatives. Juanita was born in 1978 in Guatemala. Her father was killed in war when she was three years old, and she immigrated to Los Angeles at age 14. In LA, she first lived with a family that exploited her and forced her to work, she said: “I was a village girl who was so innocent. I still have nightmares.” She later worked at a daycare near the University of California, Los Angeles, that served international families. The owner, an Iranian woman, helped take care of Juanita’s newborn son, and she grew to love Iranian festivities and food. Juanita moved to Cottage Grove with her three children in 2014. She felt safer and more at home in the “quiet and peace” of rural Oregon. She did farmwork, pruning blueberries and foraging for mushrooms. She was grateful for the fresh air, though the hours could be grueling. In 2016, the former owner of the local shop asked Juanita to take over the store. By then, she was known for helping local families with translations, with neighbors regularly asking her to interpret documents. “I didn’t want people coming into my house,” she says, with a laugh. “So this was perfect. I could have a business where I’d be helping the community.” Juanita, who got her green card in 2017, also began helping local police with interpretation. In Donald Trump’s first term, she started assisting neighbors facing deportation. One time, she recalled, she was kicked out of a federal building while trying to interpret for immigrants at their ICE check-ins: “They wanted any excuse for them to get deported. It was horrible.” She was haunted by the memory of an inconsolable three-year-old girl whose mother was detained. But as Trump returned to office, she continued to help immigrant neighbors, a practice that led her into ICE’s crosshairs. ‘I couldn’t breathe’ Around 5.30am on 5 November, Juanita awoke to a call from a friend, saying ICE had arrested a relative whose car was left behind, asking Juanita to retrieve the vehicle so it wouldn’t get towed. Juanita woke up her daughter, and the two drove in separate cars to search for the vehicle. They couldn’t find the car, and as Juanita was returning home, she spotted flashing lights behind her. She pulled over near a motel, assuming she was getting a traffic ticket from police. The men who approached drove unmarked cars, lacked visible badges, asked where she was born and said she was under arrest, she later wrote in a declaration filed as part of the lawsuit. When she responded asking why she was stopped, an officer did not show a warrant or give a reason, her declaration said. The officers reached through her window, opened the door, pulled her out and kneeled on her back, the declaration said. They also accused her of being “illegal”, she wrote. One witness video captured Juanita facedown yelling through sobs: “Why are you arresting me? You are not police … Emely! Emely!” Emely, her US citizen daughter, soon arrived. Footage Emely recorded of the incident shows she immediately explained that her mom had a green card and was carrying it on her. When she yelled to her mom that she was recording, an officer shined a bright light in her face, obscuring her camera’s view. “You don’t need to tackle her. There’s no reason to fucking do this,” Emely pleaded. The officers handcuffed Juanita and put her in their car. One officer then told Emely she could also be arrested for “obstruction”, witness video shows. The officers eventually uncuffed Juanita so she could grab her green card. When she again asked why they had arrested her and didn’t believe her, an agent responded: “Because they lie all the time,” according to Emely’s footage. Juanita was released. The encounter did not lead to charges and lasted roughly 30 minutes. One of the scariest moments, Juanita recalled, was being thrown into ICE’s vehicle “like a log”, leaving her weeping and coughing through a panic attack: “I couldn’t breathe.” While under arrest, she thought of her 12-year-old son with autism, fearing what would happen to him if she were jailed: “I said: ‘My son is at home. Don’t take me. Don’t do this.’ They didn’t listen. They didn’t care.” Juanita said she was terrified for her daughter, but added: “If Emely wasn’t there, they would have taken me.” Emely, who is studying to teach English as a second language, said after the agent shined the light in her face, she fell off a curb and badly injured her knee: “My voice was trembling and I thought if I was arrested, who is going to help my mom? … All I wanted to do was hug her.” Juanita feared agents had broken her shoulder and went to the hospital. She then visited the police station to report the incident. Forced into hiding Juanita’s arrest stunned Cottage Grove residents. “A lot of people did not believe ICE was doing these things until they saw what happened to Juanita,” said Abelio Carrillo Chales, an organizer with the Rural Organizing Project, a non-profit headquartered in town. “People were shocked and horrified, because she is well-known and has been here for so long. People were really concerned about the safety of their neighbors.” Locals organized a vigil to support Juanita and others affected by ICE and began doing early morning patrols and joining rapid response networks, Carrillo said. DHS later confirmed ICE had detained nine people in Cottage Grove that day. Three of those arrested were Carrillo’s family members, he said. One has since been deported to Guatemala; another was released and a third remains detained. The November operation, Carrillo said, sent many immigrants into hiding, regardless of their status. Children stayed home from school. Parents skipped work. Families struggled to keep up with necessities, like groceries. Carrillo, who was on the scene for Juanita’s arrest, said he was shaken by the episode and had to take time off work. He felt anguish over his inability to intervene, but knew if he got any closer, he risked detention: “I was really in fear for my life.” Emely said she had to go on medications for depression and PTSD and found herself shaking anytime a suspicious car drove by. As Juanita’s bruises faded, she became overwhelmed by the community support. She had spent her whole life helping others and didn’t know how to respond to residents leaving notes and flowers at the store and offering assistance, she said. For more than a month, her older son ran the shop while she largely stayed in bed. “My therapist said, you better get up. Go back to work. Show them you’re not defeated. The community needs you,” she said. Juanita soon was helping others affected by ICE’s crackdown, bringing food to a young girl who had stopped eating after her father was detained and picking up farmworkers who had been hiding from ICE in the woods. And she joined the lawsuit. “I was scared, because it’s the government. Who is going to win against the government?” she said. It was painful to keep retelling her account, she added: “But I told Emely, we have to tell our story. Our voices have to be heard.” ICE quotas and an app In January, Juanita’s arrest became part of a class-action suit filed by the Innovation Law Lab, an Oregon-based immigrants’ rights non-profit. Without warrants, the attorneys said, ICE can only arrest people if there is probable cause that the person is in the country without authorization and “likely to escape before a warrant can be obtained”. Instead, ICE was consistently violating people’s due-process rights, often stopping them based on their skin color, with no justification. The suit exposed details of how ICE had targeted people without warrants, with officers testifying about the Department of Homeland Security’s “Operation Black Rose”, which launched in Portland last fall and, according to DHS, led to more than 1,200 arrests through mid-December. One ICE officer testified that Black Rose teams of roughly nine to 12 officers were told to target eight arrests per day, with plaintiff’s attorneys estimating this amounted to a daily quota of 50 arrests across Oregon. The officer also revealed agents chose locations with help from a DHS app called “Elite”, which identified target areas where there might be multiple people with some “nexus” to immigration (including documented immigrants without violations). “It’s a dragnet of arresting whoever, wherever just because they look a certain way or work in a certain industry or don’t speak English,” said Nelly Garcia Orjuela, Innovation Law Lab staff attorney. Victor Cruz Gamez, the leading named plaintiff, has lived in the US for 25 years and has a work permit and protection against deportation. The father of three was nonetheless arrested by ICE in October when agents mixed him up with a different Victor Cruz, and he spent three weeks in detention before he was released. In an interview, he said he wanted to protect others and show that the government was not targeting “criminals and rapists”, as DHS has claimed. “They just see us as numbers. They don’t see us as human beings,” he said. ‘There is hope’ On 4 February, the US judge Mustafa Kasubhai sided with Innovation Law Lab, broadly halting ICE’s warrantless arrests in Oregon, saying agents could only detain people without warrants if the agents produced individualized narratives documenting the probable cause they had pre-arrest to carry out the detention. “There can be no injury more irreparable than being illegally arrested in the manner in which [plaintiffs were] stopped, detained, thrown to the ground and handcuffed,” said the judge, who said Juanita’s arrest was “without any justification”. Oregon is only the third jurisdiction with these protections, which federal judges ordered in similar cases in Colorado and Washington DC. Cruz said the ruling meant people could feel comfortable leaving their homes for the first time to get groceries or go to the doctor: “I am so relieved … There is still hope.” It’s unclear whether officers have complied with the ruling, though attorneys said there were anecdotal reports of reduced ICE activity. In Colorado, ICE has been accused of violating the federal order blocking warrantless arrests. DHS and the US justice department did not respond to inquiries about the ruling or Juanita’s arrest. Thomas Ratcliffe, the US attorney, had argued Juanita’s arrest should be excluded from the case since it was an “investigative detention”, not an arrest, but the judge rejected that argument. Ratcliffe in court also apologized to Cruz for what he had endured and expressed remorse to Emely after her testimony. He acknowledged her mother’s arrest had interfered with her final exams, saying: “I hope that you, your brother and your whole family can put this behind you.” For her part, Emely said the ruling offered some solace, though she questioned whether officers would follow it, saying: “It’s the only way my family is going to get justice.” Her family, she said, was slowly moving forward, but she has noticed changes in her mother, who was once outgoing, but is now reserved. Her mom often sits in her store, watching in fear as vehicles pass by. Emely, who recently turned 20, also said her 12-year-old brother became afraid to go to school, began lashing out and started obsessively watching ICE videos, which caused further distress. He is now in therapy, she said, and she has made sure that he never saw the footage she took of their mom’s arrest.
