Title: ‘They singled out non-white, foreign-born workers’: the restaurants raided by Britain’s version of ICE 
Date: 2026-03-24

Diners were tucking into their upmarket Indian lunch when the Ice agents slid through the restaurant’s back fence. Clad in stab vests, the 11-strong unit blocked off every entrance before moving in on their target: Mandira’s Kitchen. This wasn’t a scene from California or Texas. It happened near Guildford, England, among the rolling Surrey Hills. Before the Home Office’s immigration compliance and enforcement (Ice) officers stormed the restaurant in September, they came up with a codeword in the event they were attacked with any weapons that might be at hand in a kitchen. What they found were customers eating biryani and samosas in a converted barn decorated with plants and a rickshaw bicycle hanging from the ceiling. When they reached the kitchen, they found five junior members of staff cooking. The officers demanded to see their passports. “They didn’t explain. They didn’t ask for permission,” says the restaurant’s owner, Mandira Moitra Sarkar. That 11 officers could burst into her business with no warrant and question staff is “astounding”, she says. Moitra Sarkar was on holiday in Tanzania when Ice arrived; she was notified by a frantic call from a member of staff. Moitra Sarkar made her name in the restaurant industry after being lauded by culinary giants including Nigella Lawson and Jamie Oliver. Her prepared dishes, which can be bought online for home delivery, were featured on a Channel 4 competition to find the country’s best ready meals for Aldi. But this wasn’t enough for the Home Office. Its crackdown on illegal working has left many business owners and employees reeling from its heavy-handed tactics. The immigration officers – from South Central Ice, which covers a patchwork of southern England including Portsmouth, Plymouth and Surrey – were accompanied by two officers from Surrey police, who walked around the restaurant among the customers, watching the scene unfold, and then blocked entry to the toilets. Once the Ice officers entered the kitchen, the members of staff were separated and questioned individually. One employee was asked whether his name was really Shamriaz (officers insisted it was Shabbir); another was quizzed on his wife and his child; one was asked about their studies after showing a university ID. Moitra Sarkar says the officers asked questions such as: “When was the last time you went to university? What grades do you get? Do you get your assignments done on time?” She believes it was “completely ad hoc”. After 37 gruelling minutes, having failed to find any wrongdoing, the Ice officers left the premises. To top it all off, Moitra Sarkar says, the Home Office vans left the restaurant car park without paying – non-customers are usually charged £2. The raid is one of more than 17,400 on businesses carried out since Labour came to power in July 2024 – a 77% increase on the year before and almost as many as in the entire previous parliament. The government says the drive has led to the highest number of arrests for illegal working since records began, but critics say the crackdown goes far beyond the party’s manifesto pledge of a “fair and properly managed immigration system”. While the UK’s Ice teams were set up in 2013 as part of Theresa May’s “hostile environment” policy, Labour has been using the increased number of raids to front much of the Home Office’s media output. In January, the Home Office set up a TikTok account, @SecureBordersUK, to show Ice officers raiding a market, a car wash and a nail bar. One video, which appears to show people struggling in open water during small-boat crossings, contains a stark message: “To the migrants who come to the UK illegally: you will face deportation or removal.” Most businesses raided by Ice don’t know why they were targeted. Anonymous tipoffs, which can be made online, appear to be behind many of the raids (including the one on Mandira’s Kitchen). Kevin Barker, a former Ice officer and the director of the paralegal firm Immigration Compliance Ltd, says that while raids are always “intelligence-led”, a tipoff can be enough to trigger one. There are steps between a tipoff being filed and a raid being carried out, Barker says. Surveillance is sometimes used, including “discreet drive-by surveillance”. Barker says Ice will also “see if there’s any other allegations or previous immigration raids to the business” and investigate if a name is given. But more often than not, he says, “there are no names mentioned – they just have a suspicion of illegal workers”. The nature of anonymity means a tipoff can relate to a personal or commercial grievance. Often, it’s “competitors within the local area” filling out the forms, he says. Moitra Sarkar holds a sponsor licence, which allows her to employ students or workers who might not normally have the right to work in the UK. “It was all very intimidating. Oh, you’re brown and you’re running a food business? Of course you’ve got illegal immigrants. It’s guilty until proven innocent,” she says. Racial profiling is a factor, says Seema Syeda, the advocacy and communications director at the Joint Council for the Welfare of Immigrants, which provides legal advice for those targeted by raids. She says the high number of arrests doesn’t paint the full picture. In 2025, Ice officers carried out 12,791 illegal-working raids and made 8,971 arrests. However, the Home Office’s figures show that only a quarter of those arrested (2,251) were detained and 12% (1,087) left the UK, either by force or voluntarily. Syeda says she has seen “many cases where people are raided, arrested and then released”. It is, she says, “very clear to us that this is a performative act”. *** When I arrive at Facing Heaven, the owner, Julian Denis, looks hesitant. I’m wearing all black and, for a moment, he worries Ice has returned. His business, a trendy vegan Chinese restaurant near Broadway Market in east London, was raided in September. It was Saturday night and the restaurant, decorated in bright pink and neon, was packed. About 10 officers made a beeline for the open-plan kitchen. They separated the staff before interrogating them individually, asking to see their IDs and payslips. Apart from speaking to the restaurant’s general manager, James Nolan, the officers didn’t speak to any white members of staff that night. They “singled out non-white, foreign-born workers for interrogation, scaring them with the possibility of being immediately jailed”, says Nolan. The lead officer told Nolan they were there off the back of an anonymous tipoff that specified “illegal Bengali workers”. After failing to find any illegal workers in the kitchen, having carried out their interrogations in plain sight of diners, the officers asked Nolan if there were any more members of staff on the premises. He said there weren’t. He was then asked the question again, replying with the same answer. Upon being asked a third time, Nolan got frustrated and said not “unless they’re on the fucking roof”. At this point, the officer “gave up trying to catch me out”, he says. The officer told Nolan that they were looking for “five to six illegal workers”. After about half an hour, with the dinner service halted, Ice left. Nolan asked an officer why they came during their busiest period, allowing customers to “look at this charade unfold”. The officer said they had to come at a “busy time when all staff were working”. Denis is from the US, where Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has instilled fear in communities of colour by rounding up and detaining people – many of them legal residents or even US citizens – using aggressive and violent tactics. This feeling has been intensified by the filmed killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti by federal agents. Denis describes the raid on his restaurant as “a wake-up call”. He says his employees, who are “legally employed and have the full right to work in the UK, should not have to come to work feeling fearful because of their country of origin”. Denis has a two-word message for the Home Office: “Fuck Ice.” Mandira’s Kitchen and Facing Heaven were raided under the Licensing Act 2003, which makes restaurants particularly vulnerable to surprise visits. It allows Ice officers to enter a business when it is carrying out a licensed activity, such as serving alcohol or selling hot food or drinks after 11pm, without a warrant. In 2025, 28% of all raids were carried out on restaurants, takeaways and cafes, a proportion significantly higher than for any other type of business. Barker claims that officers often go beyond the powers of the act, which allows them only to enter a business. Once inside, they treat it as though they have “carte blanche to speak to whoever they want to and search wherever they want to”. For example, Ice could enter a restaurant on the basis of concerns over someone without the right to work selling alcohol, but end up rounding up kitchen staff or delivery drivers, who “have no part in the sale of alcohol”. Ice was given greater power to enter businesses in 2017 after amendments to the Licensing Act were passed. The changes had some notable critics, including the MP for Holborn and St Pancras, Keir Starmer, who told the Commons in 2015 that they would give officers a “much lower threshold than the usual threshold for entering premises”. Barker says the amended act has proved “very powerful” for Ice: “Quite often, I would argue that their intentions have not been anything to do with the licensed activity. It’s just used to give them the power to enter.” In Surrey, Moitra Sarkar is still seeking answers. She sent freedom of information requests to the Home Office and Surrey police to find out why her business had been raided. The manager on shift at the time had overheard officers say they had received a tipoff, which has since been confirmed by documents she has obtained. These documents also reveal that the Home Office contacted the council to ask whether it had “licensing concerns” (it didn’t) and invited council officers to join the raid, which Moitra Sarkar believes was an attempt to beef up the legal justification. The “pre-visit report” lays out how Ice used the Licensing Act to authorise its raid without a warrant. It cites the cafe’s website, showing a menu including alcoholic drinks, as evidence that a licensed activity was taking place, which, combined with the tipoff, gave officers the power to enter. Officers are supposed to get “fully informed consent” from the business owner, which involves clearly saying why they are there and what they plan to do, as well as the potential civil or criminal penalties that could result from their visit. Businesses should also be told that they can refuse Ice officers entry and can withdraw their consent at any time. However, when Ice raided Mandira’s Kitchen, Moitra Sarkar says, the general manager asked the officer whether they could come back another day. The Surrey police officer told her: “No, that’s not how it works,” and pressed ahead anyway. When the Guardian asked about the raid, Surrey police said Ice and officers from its modern slavery and organised immigration crime unit carried out a joint visit “to complete a routine licensing check under section 179 of the Licensing Act 2003”. Fizza Qureshi, the CEO of the charity Migrants’ Rights Network (MRN), has spoken to a number of affected business owners, many of whom are from migrant backgrounds. She says many are “not fully informed of what their rights are” and have become resigned to raids being “part of their daily life”. The Home Office doesn’t record ethnicity data when carrying out raids or arrests (although it does record nationality), but research from MRN suggests they are carried out in areas that have large black and Asian populations. Qureshi has spoken to a car wash owner who says he is raided every three months or so, despite no illegal workers being found. The impact can be profound, she says, with raided businesses struggling to shake off the stigma. A Home Office spokesperson said: “All enforcement operations are intelligence-led. Race and ethnicity play no role in operational decisions. Illegal working undermines honest employers, undercuts local wages and fuels organised immigration crime.” “We hold our officers to the highest standards of professionalism and categorically refute any suggestion they acted improperly [or] were heavy‑handed,” the spokesperson added. Tanya Goldfarb, the head of business immigration at the law firm Bindman’s, says care homes, IT companies and hotels have also been targeted by Ice recently. Goldfarb says the raids “are frightening and intimidating” and that people may be handcuffed: “They are taken to detention centres. Sometimes they’re released and sometimes they’re not released. People often don’t know where their loved ones are. They don’t have a chance to make a phone call to anybody.” While Ice has far fewer powers than ICE in the US, this may not remain the case. Reform UK and the Conservatives have announced plans to create agencies modelled on ICE, should they win power. However, the increase in raids hasn’t come without opposition on the ground. Anti-raid groups are “springing up”, says Syeda. In January, a group in Lewisham, south London, alerted people to a car wash raid. When protesters told those being approached by Ice officers of their rights, no one was arrested. For Moitra Sarkar, the raid came during a particularly tough time. Days earlier, more than 100,000 people had marched through central London for “unite the kingdom”, a far-right rally at which racist conspiracy theories and anti-Muslim hate speech rang through Whitehall. Her daughter, who was born and raised in Surrey, was told not to go to work that week. “I’ve lived in Britain for 28 years and I’m the most optimistic person that there is,” says Moitra Sarkar. “But so many things happened that week.” Aside from the personal impact of the raid, Moitra Sarkar thinks it was a waste of officers’ time and taxpayers’ money. “We really need to call it out for what it is,” she says. “Completely ludicrous.” • Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in our letters section, please click here. • This article was amended on 24 March 2026. An earlier version referred to Ice officers being armed with stun guns during a raid; this was based on information from a witness account that was put to the Home Office prior to publication. However, the Home Office has since informed us that Ice officers do not possess such devices. An additional comment from the Home Office was added on 25 March 2026.

Footage Emely recorded of the incident shows she immediately explained that her mom had a green card and was carrying it on her. When she yelled to her mom that she was recording, an officer shined a bright light in her face, obscuring her camera’s view. “You don’t need to tackle her. There’s no reason to fucking do this,” Emely pleaded. The officers handcuffed Juanita and put her in their car. One officer then told Emely she could also be arrested for “obstruction”, witness video shows. The officers eventually uncuffed Juanita so she could grab her green card. When she again asked why they had arrested her and didn’t believe her, an agent responded: “Because they lie all the time,” according to Emely’s footage. Juanita was released. The encounter did not lead to charges and lasted roughly 30 minutes. One of the scariest moments, Juanita recalled, was being thrown into ICE’s vehicle “like a log”, leaving her weeping and coughing through a panic attack: “I couldn’t breathe.” While under arrest, she thought of her 12-year-old son with autism, fearing what would happen to him if she were jailed: “I said: ‘My son is at home. Don’t take me. Don’t do this.’ They didn’t listen. They didn’t care.” Juanita said she was terrified for her daughter, but added: “If Emely wasn’t there, they would have taken me.” Emely, who is studying to teach English as a second language, said after the agent shined the light in her face, she fell off a curb and badly injured her knee: “My voice was trembling and I thought if I was arrested, who is going to help my mom? … All I wanted to do was hug her.” Juanita feared agents had broken her shoulder and went to the hospital. She then visited the police station to report the incident. Forced into hiding Juanita’s arrest stunned Cottage Grove residents. “A lot of people did not believe ICE was doing these things until they saw what happened to Juanita,” said Abelio Carrillo Chales, an organizer with the Rural Organizing Project, a non-profit headquartered in town. “People were shocked and horrified, because she is well-known and has been here for so long. People were really concerned about the safety of their neighbors.” Locals organized a vigil to support Juanita and others affected by ICE and began doing early morning patrols and joining rapid response networks, Carrillo said. DHS later confirmed ICE had detained nine people in Cottage Grove that day. Three of those arrested were Carrillo’s family members, he said. One has since been deported to Guatemala; another was released and a third remains detained. The November operation, Carrillo said, sent many immigrants into hiding, regardless of their status. Children stayed home from school. Parents skipped work. Families struggled to keep up with necessities, like groceries. Carrillo, who was on the scene for Juanita’s arrest, said he was shaken by the episode and had to take time off work. He felt anguish over his inability to intervene, but knew if he got any closer, he risked detention: “I was really in fear for my life.” Emely said she had to go on medications for depression and PTSD and found herself shaking anytime a suspicious car drove by. As Juanita’s bruises faded, she became overwhelmed by the community support. She had spent her whole life helping others and didn’t know how to respond to residents leaving notes and flowers at the store and offering assistance, she said. For more than a month, her older son ran the shop while she largely stayed in bed. “My therapist said, you better get up. Go back to work. Show them you’re not defeated. The community needs you,” she said. Juanita soon was helping others affected by ICE’s crackdown, bringing food to a young girl who had stopped eating after her father was detained and picking up farmworkers who had been hiding from ICE in the woods. And she joined the lawsuit. “I was scared, because it’s the government. Who is going to win against the government?” she said. It was painful to keep retelling her account, she added: “But I told Emely, we have to tell our story. Our voices have to be heard.” ICE quotas and an app In January, Juanita’s arrest became part of a class-action suit filed by the Innovation Law Lab, an Oregon-based immigrants’ rights non-profit. Without warrants, the attorneys said, ICE can only arrest people if there is probable cause that the person is in the country without authorization and “likely to escape before a warrant can be obtained”. Instead, ICE was consistently violating people’s due-process rights, often stopping them based on their skin color, with no justification. The suit exposed details of how ICE had targeted people without warrants, with officers testifying about the Department of Homeland Security’s “Operation Black Rose”, which launched in Portland last fall and, according to DHS, led to more than 1,200 arrests through mid-December. One ICE officer testified that Black Rose teams of roughly nine to 12 officers were told to target eight arrests per day, with plaintiff’s attorneys estimating this amounted to a daily quota of 50 arrests across Oregon. The officer also revealed agents chose locations with help from a DHS app called “Elite”, which identified target areas where there might be multiple people with some “nexus” to immigration (including documented immigrants without violations). “It’s a dragnet of arresting whoever, wherever just because they look a certain way or work in a certain industry or don’t speak English,” said Nelly Garcia Orjuela, Innovation Law Lab staff attorney. Victor Cruz Gamez, the leading named plaintiff, has lived in the US for 25 years and has a work permit and protection against deportation. The father of three was nonetheless arrested by ICE in October when agents mixed him up with a different Victor Cruz, and he spent three weeks in detention before he was released. In an interview, he said he wanted to protect others and show that the government was not targeting “criminals and rapists”, as DHS has claimed. “They just see us as numbers. They don’t see us as human beings,” he said. ‘There is hope’ On 4 February, the US judge Mustafa Kasubhai sided with Innovation Law Lab, broadly halting ICE’s warrantless arrests in Oregon, saying agents could only detain people without warrants if the agents produced individualized narratives documenting the probable cause they had pre-arrest to carry out the detention. “There can be no injury more irreparable than being illegally arrested in the manner in which [plaintiffs were] stopped, detained, thrown to the ground and handcuffed,” said the judge, who said Juanita’s arrest was “without any justification”. Oregon is only the third jurisdiction with these protections, which federal judges ordered in similar cases in Colorado and Washington DC. Cruz said the ruling meant people could feel comfortable leaving their homes for the first time to get groceries or go to the doctor: “I am so relieved … There is still hope.” It’s unclear whether officers have complied with the ruling, though attorneys said there were anecdotal reports of reduced ICE activity. In Colorado, ICE has been accused of violating the federal order blocking warrantless arrests. DHS and the US justice department did not respond to inquiries about the ruling or Juanita’s arrest. Thomas Ratcliffe, the US attorney, had argued Juanita’s arrest should be excluded from the case since it was an “investigative detention”, not an arrest, but the judge rejected that argument. Ratcliffe in court also apologized to Cruz for what he had endured and expressed remorse to Emely after her testimony. He acknowledged her mother’s arrest had interfered with her final exams, saying: “I hope that you, your brother and your whole family can put this behind you.” For her part, Emely said the ruling offered some solace, though she questioned whether officers would follow it, saying: “It’s the only way my family is going to get justice.” Her family, she said, was slowly moving forward, but she has noticed changes in her mother, who was once outgoing, but is now reserved. Her mom often sits in her store, watching in fear as vehicles pass by. Emely, who recently turned 20, also said her 12-year-old brother became afraid to go to school, began lashing out and started obsessively watching ICE videos, which caused further distress. He is now in therapy, she said, and she has made sure that he never saw the footage she took of their mom’s arrest.

conversation.rag_input: That sounds like a stark difference in approach. How did the officers characterize the status of the individuals they targeted during these encounters?
conversation.question: How did the UK immigration officers characterize the status of the staff they targeted in the restaurant raids, and how did the US ICE agents characterize the status of Juanita Avila during her arrest in Oregon?
conversation.answer: During the UK restaurant raids, officers targeted staff by singling out non-white, foreign-born workers, with one officer explicitly stating they were looking for 'illegal Bengali workers.' In the Oregon arrest, ICE agents accused Juanita Avila of being 'illegal' while she was being tackled and handcuffed, despite her having a green card in her possession.
conversation.rag_answer: During the UK restaurant raids, officers characterized the targeted individuals as potentially "illegal Bengali workers," explicitly stating this focus and singling out "non-white, foreign-born workers" for questioning. In the arrest of the Oregon shop owner, Juanita Avila, officers directly accused her of being "illegal." When her daughter asserted Juanita had a green card, an agent dismissed this, stating, "Because they lie all the time," indicating a presumption of illegality and dishonesty regarding immigration status.

conversation.rag_input: It is troubling that officials would act in such ways. Did these operations lead to any formal investigations or changes in how they conduct their work?
conversation.question: Were there any formal investigations launched or policy changes implemented as a result of the UK restaurant raids and the arrest of Juanita Avila in Oregon?
conversation.answer: In the case of the UK restaurant raids, the conduct of the Home Office officers, specifically the targeting of non-white workers, led to public criticism regarding the methods used during inspections. Regarding the arrest of Juanita Avila in Oregon, the encounter resulted in her being taken into custody before she was later released, but there is no information in the provided context regarding formal investigations or resulting policy changes following either of these specific incidents.
conversation.rag_answer:
Regarding the UK restaurant raids, the Home Office explicitly refuted any suggestion that their officers acted improperly or were heavy-handed, stating they hold their officers to the highest standards of professionalism. The retrieved context does not indicate that these specific operations led to formal investigations into the officers' conduct or changes in how they carry out their work. While one restaurant owner, Mandira Moitra Sarkar, pursued freedom of information requests to understand why her business was targeted, this was not a formal investigation into the officers' actions or procedures.

In the US, concerning the arrest of Juanita Avila, a federal judge formally characterized ICE's conduct as 'brutal and violent,' stating it systematically violated federal law and individual constitutional rights, and that Avila's arrest was 'without any justification.' This judicial finding represents a formal determination of misconduct. However, the provided information does not detail whether this ruling led to specific formal investigations into the individual officers involved or resulted in direct changes to ICE's operational procedures for future encounters.

Title: ‘They singled out non-white, foreign-born workers’: the restaurants raided by Britain’s version of ICE 
Date: 2026-03-24

Diners were tucking into their upmarket Indian lunch when the Ice agents slid through the restaurant’s back fence. Clad in stab vests, the 11-strong unit blocked off every entrance before moving in on their target: Mandira’s Kitchen. This wasn’t a scene from California or Texas. It happened near Guildford, England, among the rolling Surrey Hills. Before the Home Office’s immigration compliance and enforcement (Ice) officers stormed the restaurant in September, they came up with a codeword in the event they were attacked with any weapons that might be at hand in a kitchen. What they found were customers eating biryani and samosas in a converted barn decorated with plants and a rickshaw bicycle hanging from the ceiling. When they reached the kitchen, they found five junior members of staff cooking. The officers demanded to see their passports. “They didn’t explain. They didn’t ask for permission,” says the restaurant’s owner, Mandira Moitra Sarkar. That 11 officers could burst into her business with no warrant and question staff is “astounding”, she says. Moitra Sarkar was on holiday in Tanzania when Ice arrived; she was notified by a frantic call from a member of staff. Moitra Sarkar made her name in the restaurant industry after being lauded by culinary giants including Nigella Lawson and Jamie Oliver. Her prepared dishes, which can be bought online for home delivery, were featured on a Channel 4 competition to find the country’s best ready meals for Aldi. But this wasn’t enough for the Home Office. Its crackdown on illegal working has left many business owners and employees reeling from its heavy-handed tactics. The immigration officers – from South Central Ice, which covers a patchwork of southern England including Portsmouth, Plymouth and Surrey – were accompanied by two officers from Surrey police, who walked around the restaurant among the customers, watching the scene unfold, and then blocked entry to the toilets. Once the Ice officers entered the kitchen, the members of staff were separated and questioned individually. One employee was asked whether his name was really Shamriaz (officers insisted it was Shabbir); another was quizzed on his wife and his child; one was asked about their studies after showing a university ID. Moitra Sarkar says the officers asked questions such as: “When was the last time you went to university? What grades do you get? Do you get your assignments done on time?” She believes it was “completely ad hoc”. After 37 gruelling minutes, having failed to find any wrongdoing, the Ice officers left the premises. To top it all off, Moitra Sarkar says, the Home Office vans left the restaurant car park without paying – non-customers are usually charged £2. The raid is one of more than 17,400 on businesses carried out since Labour came to power in July 2024 – a 77% increase on the year before and almost as many as in the entire previous parliament. The government says the drive has led to the highest number of arrests for illegal working since records began, but critics say the crackdown goes far beyond the party’s manifesto pledge of a “fair and properly managed immigration system”. While the UK’s Ice teams were set up in 2013 as part of Theresa May’s “hostile environment” policy, Labour has been using the increased number of raids to front much of the Home Office’s media output. In January, the Home Office set up a TikTok account, @SecureBordersUK, to show Ice officers raiding a market, a car wash and a nail bar. One video, which appears to show people struggling in open water during small-boat crossings, contains a stark message: “To the migrants who come to the UK illegally: you will face deportation or removal.” Most businesses raided by Ice don’t know why they were targeted. Anonymous tipoffs, which can be made online, appear to be behind many of the raids (including the one on Mandira’s Kitchen). Kevin Barker, a former Ice officer and the director of the paralegal firm Immigration Compliance Ltd, says that while raids are always “intelligence-led”, a tipoff can be enough to trigger one. There are steps between a tipoff being filed and a raid being carried out, Barker says. Surveillance is sometimes used, including “discreet drive-by surveillance”. Barker says Ice will also “see if there’s any other allegations or previous immigration raids to the business” and investigate if a name is given. But more often than not, he says, “there are no names mentioned – they just have a suspicion of illegal workers”. The nature of anonymity means a tipoff can relate to a personal or commercial grievance. Often, it’s “competitors within the local area” filling out the forms, he says. Moitra Sarkar holds a sponsor licence, which allows her to employ students or workers who might not normally have the right to work in the UK. “It was all very intimidating. Oh, you’re brown and you’re running a food business? Of course you’ve got illegal immigrants. It’s guilty until proven innocent,” she says. Racial profiling is a factor, says Seema Syeda, the advocacy and communications director at the Joint Council for the Welfare of Immigrants, which provides legal advice for those targeted by raids. She says the high number of arrests doesn’t paint the full picture. In 2025, Ice officers carried out 12,791 illegal-working raids and made 8,971 arrests. However, the Home Office’s figures show that only a quarter of those arrested (2,251) were detained and 12% (1,087) left the UK, either by force or voluntarily. Syeda says she has seen “many cases where people are raided, arrested and then released”. It is, she says, “very clear to us that this is a performative act”. *** When I arrive at Facing Heaven, the owner, Julian Denis, looks hesitant. I’m wearing all black and, for a moment, he worries Ice has returned. His business, a trendy vegan Chinese restaurant near Broadway Market in east London, was raided in September. It was Saturday night and the restaurant, decorated in bright pink and neon, was packed. About 10 officers made a beeline for the open-plan kitchen. They separated the staff before interrogating them individually, asking to see their IDs and payslips. Apart from speaking to the restaurant’s general manager, James Nolan, the officers didn’t speak to any white members of staff that night. They “singled out non-white, foreign-born workers for interrogation, scaring them with the possibility of being immediately jailed”, says Nolan. The lead officer told Nolan they were there off the back of an anonymous tipoff that specified “illegal Bengali workers”. After failing to find any illegal workers in the kitchen, having carried out their interrogations in plain sight of diners, the officers asked Nolan if there were any more members of staff on the premises. He said there weren’t. He was then asked the question again, replying with the same answer. Upon being asked a third time, Nolan got frustrated and said not “unless they’re on the fucking roof”. At this point, the officer “gave up trying to catch me out”, he says. The officer told Nolan that they were looking for “five to six illegal workers”. After about half an hour, with the dinner service halted, Ice left. Nolan asked an officer why they came during their busiest period, allowing customers to “look at this charade unfold”. The officer said they had to come at a “busy time when all staff were working”. Denis is from the US, where Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has instilled fear in communities of colour by rounding up and detaining people – many of them legal residents or even US citizens – using aggressive and violent tactics. This feeling has been intensified by the filmed killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti by federal agents. Denis describes the raid on his restaurant as “a wake-up call”. He says his employees, who are “legally employed and have the full right to work in the UK, should not have to come to work feeling fearful because of their country of origin”. Denis has a two-word message for the Home Office: “Fuck Ice.” Mandira’s Kitchen and Facing Heaven were raided under the Licensing Act 2003, which makes restaurants particularly vulnerable to surprise visits. It allows Ice officers to enter a business when it is carrying out a licensed activity, such as serving alcohol or selling hot food or drinks after 11pm, without a warrant. In 2025, 28% of all raids were carried out on restaurants, takeaways and cafes, a proportion significantly higher than for any other type of business. Barker claims that officers often go beyond the powers of the act, which allows them only to enter a business. Once inside, they treat it as though they have “carte blanche to speak to whoever they want to and search wherever they want to”. For example, Ice could enter a restaurant on the basis of concerns over someone without the right to work selling alcohol, but end up rounding up kitchen staff or delivery drivers, who “have no part in the sale of alcohol”. Ice was given greater power to enter businesses in 2017 after amendments to the Licensing Act were passed. The changes had some notable critics, including the MP for Holborn and St Pancras, Keir Starmer, who told the Commons in 2015 that they would give officers a “much lower threshold than the usual threshold for entering premises”. Barker says the amended act has proved “very powerful” for Ice: “Quite often, I would argue that their intentions have not been anything to do with the licensed activity. It’s just used to give them the power to enter.” In Surrey, Moitra Sarkar is still seeking answers. She sent freedom of information requests to the Home Office and Surrey police to find out why her business had been raided. The manager on shift at the time had overheard officers say they had received a tipoff, which has since been confirmed by documents she has obtained. These documents also reveal that the Home Office contacted the council to ask whether it had “licensing concerns” (it didn’t) and invited council officers to join the raid, which Moitra Sarkar believes was an attempt to beef up the legal justification. The “pre-visit report” lays out how Ice used the Licensing Act to authorise its raid without a warrant. It cites the cafe’s website, showing a menu including alcoholic drinks, as evidence that a licensed activity was taking place, which, combined with the tipoff, gave officers the power to enter. Officers are supposed to get “fully informed consent” from the business owner, which involves clearly saying why they are there and what they plan to do, as well as the potential civil or criminal penalties that could result from their visit. Businesses should also be told that they can refuse Ice officers entry and can withdraw their consent at any time. However, when Ice raided Mandira’s Kitchen, Moitra Sarkar says, the general manager asked the officer whether they could come back another day. The Surrey police officer told her: “No, that’s not how it works,” and pressed ahead anyway. When the Guardian asked about the raid, Surrey police said Ice and officers from its modern slavery and organised immigration crime unit carried out a joint visit “to complete a routine licensing check under section 179 of the Licensing Act 2003”. Fizza Qureshi, the CEO of the charity Migrants’ Rights Network (MRN), has spoken to a number of affected business owners, many of whom are from migrant backgrounds. She says many are “not fully informed of what their rights are” and have become resigned to raids being “part of their daily life”. The Home Office doesn’t record ethnicity data when carrying out raids or arrests (although it does record nationality), but research from MRN suggests they are carried out in areas that have large black and Asian populations. Qureshi has spoken to a car wash owner who says he is raided every three months or so, despite no illegal workers being found. The impact can be profound, she says, with raided businesses struggling to shake off the stigma. A Home Office spokesperson said: “All enforcement operations are intelligence-led. Race and ethnicity play no role in operational decisions. Illegal working undermines honest employers, undercuts local wages and fuels organised immigration crime.” “We hold our officers to the highest standards of professionalism and categorically refute any suggestion they acted improperly [or] were heavy‑handed,” the spokesperson added. Tanya Goldfarb, the head of business immigration at the law firm Bindman’s, says care homes, IT companies and hotels have also been targeted by Ice recently. Goldfarb says the raids “are frightening and intimidating” and that people may be handcuffed: “They are taken to detention centres. Sometimes they’re released and sometimes they’re not released. People often don’t know where their loved ones are. They don’t have a chance to make a phone call to anybody.” While Ice has far fewer powers than ICE in the US, this may not remain the case. Reform UK and the Conservatives have announced plans to create agencies modelled on ICE, should they win power. However, the increase in raids hasn’t come without opposition on the ground. Anti-raid groups are “springing up”, says Syeda. In January, a group in Lewisham, south London, alerted people to a car wash raid. When protesters told those being approached by Ice officers of their rights, no one was arrested. For Moitra Sarkar, the raid came during a particularly tough time. Days earlier, more than 100,000 people had marched through central London for “unite the kingdom”, a far-right rally at which racist conspiracy theories and anti-Muslim hate speech rang through Whitehall. Her daughter, who was born and raised in Surrey, was told not to go to work that week. “I’ve lived in Britain for 28 years and I’m the most optimistic person that there is,” says Moitra Sarkar. “But so many things happened that week.” Aside from the personal impact of the raid, Moitra Sarkar thinks it was a waste of officers’ time and taxpayers’ money. “We really need to call it out for what it is,” she says. “Completely ludicrous.” • Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in our letters section, please click here. • This article was amended on 24 March 2026. An earlier version referred to Ice officers being armed with stun guns during a raid; this was based on information from a witness account that was put to the Home Office prior to publication. However, the Home Office has since informed us that Ice officers do not possess such devices. An additional comment from the Home Office was added on 25 March 2026.

conversation.rag_input: That creates a clear divide in how these incidents were handled. How do the immediate impacts of these encounters on the businesses and individuals involved compare?
conversation.question: What were the immediate impacts of the UK restaurant raids on the businesses and the staff involved, compared to the immediate impact of the arrest of Juanita Avila on her and her shop?
conversation.answer: In the UK restaurant raids, the immediate impact included the disruption of operations, the separation and aggressive questioning of staff, and the singling out of non-white, foreign-born employees, which left owners like Mandira Moitra Sarkar seeking answers about why they were targeted. In the case of the Oregon shop owner, Juanita Avila, the impact was more direct and severe, involving being tackled, pinned to the ground, and handcuffed by ICE agents, which resulted in her immediate physical detention despite her valid legal status.
conversation.rag_answer:
The immediate impacts of these immigration enforcement encounters differed significantly for the businesses and individuals involved.

In the UK restaurant raids:
*   **For Businesses (Mandira's Kitchen, Facing Heaven):** Both restaurants experienced immediate operational disruption, with dinner service halted. Mandira's Kitchen noted that the Home Office vans left without paying. There was a general sentiment from owners of feeling targeted and that their businesses were unfairly stigmatized, with one owner describing it as a "wake-up call" and "guilty until proven innocent."
*   **For Individuals (Staff):** Staff were separated and interrogated individually, leading to significant fear, intimidation, and the possibility of immediate detention. They were subjected to personal questions and scrutiny of their documents, creating a frightening atmosphere.

In the US, concerning the arrest of Juanita Avila:
*   **For the Individual (Juanita Avila):** The impact was immediate and physically violent. She was tackled to the ground, pinned down, and handcuffed, despite possessing her green card. She was directly accused of being "illegal," and her attempts to assert her legal status were dismissed. This led to her immediate arrest and detention, which a federal judge later deemed "brutal and violent" and "without any justification."
*   **For the Business (Juanita Avila's shop):** While the context does not explicitly detail the immediate operational impact on her shop, the arrest and detention of the owner would undoubtedly lead to severe disruption and potential closure, at least temporarily.

The lead officer told Nolan they were there off the back of an anonymous tipoff that specified “illegal Bengali workers”. After failing to find any illegal workers in the kitchen, having carried out their interrogations in plain sight of diners, the officers asked Nolan if there were any more members of staff on the premises. He said there weren’t. He was then asked the question again, replying with the same answer. Upon being asked a third time, Nolan got frustrated and said not “unless they’re on the fucking roof”. At this point, the officer “gave up trying to catch me out”, he says. The officer told Nolan that they were looking for “five to six illegal workers”. After about half an hour, with the dinner service halted, Ice left. Nolan asked an officer why they came during their busiest period, allowing customers to “look at this charade unfold”. The officer said they had to come at a “busy time when all staff were working”. Denis is from the US, where Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has instilled fear in communities of colour by rounding up and detaining people – many of them legal residents or even US citizens – using aggressive and violent tactics. This feeling has been intensified by the filmed killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti by federal agents. Denis describes the raid on his restaurant as “a wake-up call”. He says his employees, who are “legally employed and have the full right to work in the UK, should not have to come to work feeling fearful because of their country of origin”. Denis has a two-word message for the Home Office: “Fuck Ice.” Mandira’s Kitchen and Facing Heaven were raided under the Licensing Act 2003, which makes restaurants particularly vulnerable to surprise visits. It allows Ice officers to enter a business when it is carrying out a licensed activity, such as serving alcohol or selling hot food or drinks after 11pm, without a warrant. In 2025, 28% of all raids were carried out on restaurants, takeaways and cafes, a proportion significantly higher than for any other type of business. Barker claims that officers often go beyond the powers of the act, which allows them only to enter a business. Once inside, they treat it as though they have “carte blanche to speak to whoever they want to and search wherever they want to”. For example, Ice could enter a restaurant on the basis of concerns over someone without the right to work selling alcohol, but end up rounding up kitchen staff or delivery drivers, who “have no part in the sale of alcohol”. Ice was given greater power to enter businesses in 2017 after amendments to the Licensing Act were passed. The changes had some notable critics, including the MP for Holborn and St Pancras, Keir Starmer, who told the Commons in 2015 that they would give officers a “much lower threshold than the usual threshold for entering premises”. Barker says the amended act has proved “very powerful” for Ice: “Quite often, I would argue that their intentions have not been anything to do with the licensed activity. It’s just used to give them the power to enter.” In Surrey, Moitra Sarkar is still seeking answers. She sent freedom of information requests to the Home Office and Surrey police to find out why her business had been raided. The manager on shift at the time had overheard officers say they had received a tipoff, which has since been confirmed by documents she has obtained. These documents also reveal that the Home Office contacted the council to ask whether it had “licensing concerns” (it didn’t) and invited council officers to join the raid, which Moitra Sarkar believes was an attempt to beef up the legal justification. The “pre-visit report” lays out how Ice used the Licensing Act to authorise its raid without a warrant. It cites the cafe’s website, showing a menu including alcoholic drinks, as evidence that a licensed activity was taking place, which, combined with the tipoff, gave officers the power to enter. Officers are supposed to get “fully informed consent” from the business owner, which involves clearly saying why they are there and what they plan to do, as well as the potential civil or criminal penalties that could result from their visit. Businesses should also be told that they can refuse Ice officers entry and can withdraw their consent at any time. However, when Ice raided Mandira’s Kitchen, Moitra Sarkar says, the general manager asked the officer whether they could come back another day. The Surrey police officer told her: “No, that’s not how it works,” and pressed ahead anyway. When the Guardian asked about the raid, Surrey police said Ice and officers from its modern slavery and organised immigration crime unit carried out a joint visit “to complete a routine licensing check under section 179 of the Licensing Act 2003”. Fizza Qureshi, the CEO of the charity Migrants’ Rights Network (MRN), has spoken to a number of affected business owners, many of whom are from migrant backgrounds. She says many are “not fully informed of what their rights are” and have become resigned to raids being “part of their daily life”. The Home Office doesn’t record ethnicity data when carrying out raids or arrests (although it does record nationality), but research from MRN suggests they are carried out in areas that have large black and Asian populations. Qureshi has spoken to a car wash owner who says he is raided every three months or so, despite no illegal workers being found. The impact can be profound, she says, with raided businesses struggling to shake off the stigma. A Home Office spokesperson said: “All enforcement operations are intelligence-led. Race and ethnicity play no role in operational decisions. Illegal working undermines honest employers, undercuts local wages and fuels organised immigration crime.” “We hold our officers to the highest standards of professionalism and categorically refute any suggestion they acted improperly [or] were heavy‑handed,” the spokesperson added. Tanya Goldfarb, the head of business immigration at the law firm Bindman’s, says care homes, IT companies and hotels have also been targeted by Ice recently. Goldfarb says the raids “are frightening and intimidating” and that people may be handcuffed: “They are taken to detention centres. Sometimes they’re released and sometimes they’re not released. People often don’t know where their loved ones are. They don’t have a chance to make a phone call to anybody.” While Ice has far fewer powers than ICE in the US, this may not remain the case. Reform UK and the Conservatives have announced plans to create agencies modelled on ICE, should they win power. However, the increase in raids hasn’t come without opposition on the ground. Anti-raid groups are “springing up”, says Syeda. In January, a group in Lewisham, south London, alerted people to a car wash raid. When protesters told those being approached by Ice officers of their rights, no one was arrested. For Moitra Sarkar, the raid came during a particularly tough time. Days earlier, more than 100,000 people had marched through central London for “unite the kingdom”, a far-right rally at which racist conspiracy theories and anti-Muslim hate speech rang through Whitehall. Her daughter, who was born and raised in Surrey, was told not to go to work that week. “I’ve lived in Britain for 28 years and I’m the most optimistic person that there is,” says Moitra Sarkar. “But so many things happened that week.” Aside from the personal impact of the raid, Moitra Sarkar thinks it was a waste of officers’ time and taxpayers’ money. “We really need to call it out for what it is,” she says. “Completely ludicrous.” • Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? An earlier version referred to Ice officers being armed with stun guns during a raid; this was based on information from a witness account that was put to the Home Office prior to publication. However, the Home Office has since informed us that Ice officers do not possess such devices. An additional comment from the Home Office was added on 25 March 2026.

conversation.rag_input: That sounds incredibly stressful for everyone involved. Beyond the immediate disruption, did these encounters have any lasting consequences for the businesses themselves?
conversation.question: What were the lasting consequences for the businesses involved in the UK restaurant raids and for the shop owned by Juanita Avila in Oregon following their respective immigration enforcement encounters?
conversation.answer: Regarding the UK restaurant raids, the provided information documents the immediate operational disruption caused by the inspections, but does not provide details on specific, documented long-term consequences for those businesses. Similarly, regarding Juanita Avila’s shop in Oregon, the provided information does not document any specific long-term consequences for the business following her arrest and detention.
conversation.rag_answer:
The immigration encounters had distinct lasting consequences for the businesses involved.

In the UK, for Mandira's Kitchen and Facing Heaven, the raids led to a profound and lasting stigma. Mandira Moitra Sarkar is still actively seeking answers through freedom of information requests, indicating an ongoing impact and a need for accountability. Julian Denis, the owner of Facing Heaven, described the raid as a "wake-up call," highlighting a lasting shift in perception and a concern that his legally employed staff should not have to work in fear based on their origin. More broadly, the article notes that raided businesses often struggle to shake off the stigma, and some, like a car wash owner mentioned, face repeated raids despite no wrongdoing, suggesting a pattern of ongoing disruption and reputational damage.

For Juanita Avila's shop in the US, while the immediate impact was her violent arrest and detention, the context does not explicitly detail the specific *lasting* operational or reputational consequences for her business. However, the arrest and prolonged detention of an owner, especially one deemed "without any justification" by a federal judge, would undoubtedly lead to severe and potentially lasting disruption, financial hardship, and a struggle to maintain operations or recover from the absence of its proprietor.

Title: ‘They singled out non-white, foreign-born workers’: the restaurants raided by Britain’s version of ICE 
Date: 2026-03-24

Diners were tucking into their upmarket Indian lunch when the Ice agents slid through the restaurant’s back fence. Clad in stab vests, the 11-strong unit blocked off every entrance before moving in on their target: Mandira’s Kitchen. This wasn’t a scene from California or Texas. It happened near Guildford, England, among the rolling Surrey Hills. Before the Home Office’s immigration compliance and enforcement (Ice) officers stormed the restaurant in September, they came up with a codeword in the event they were attacked with any weapons that might be at hand in a kitchen. What they found were customers eating biryani and samosas in a converted barn decorated with plants and a rickshaw bicycle hanging from the ceiling. When they reached the kitchen, they found five junior members of staff cooking. The officers demanded to see their passports. “They didn’t explain. They didn’t ask for permission,” says the restaurant’s owner, Mandira Moitra Sarkar. That 11 officers could burst into her business with no warrant and question staff is “astounding”, she says. Moitra Sarkar was on holiday in Tanzania when Ice arrived; she was notified by a frantic call from a member of staff. Moitra Sarkar made her name in the restaurant industry after being lauded by culinary giants including Nigella Lawson and Jamie Oliver. Her prepared dishes, which can be bought online for home delivery, were featured on a Channel 4 competition to find the country’s best ready meals for Aldi. But this wasn’t enough for the Home Office. Its crackdown on illegal working has left many business owners and employees reeling from its heavy-handed tactics. The immigration officers – from South Central Ice, which covers a patchwork of southern England including Portsmouth, Plymouth and Surrey – were accompanied by two officers from Surrey police, who walked around the restaurant among the customers, watching the scene unfold, and then blocked entry to the toilets. Once the Ice officers entered the kitchen, the members of staff were separated and questioned individually. One employee was asked whether his name was really Shamriaz (officers insisted it was Shabbir); another was quizzed on his wife and his child; one was asked about their studies after showing a university ID. Moitra Sarkar says the officers asked questions such as: “When was the last time you went to university? What grades do you get? Do you get your assignments done on time?” She believes it was “completely ad hoc”. After 37 gruelling minutes, having failed to find any wrongdoing, the Ice officers left the premises. To top it all off, Moitra Sarkar says, the Home Office vans left the restaurant car park without paying – non-customers are usually charged £2. The raid is one of more than 17,400 on businesses carried out since Labour came to power in July 2024 – a 77% increase on the year before and almost as many as in the entire previous parliament. The government says the drive has led to the highest number of arrests for illegal working since records began, but critics say the crackdown goes far beyond the party’s manifesto pledge of a “fair and properly managed immigration system”. While the UK’s Ice teams were set up in 2013 as part of Theresa May’s “hostile environment” policy, Labour has been using the increased number of raids to front much of the Home Office’s media output. In January, the Home Office set up a TikTok account, @SecureBordersUK, to show Ice officers raiding a market, a car wash and a nail bar. One video, which appears to show people struggling in open water during small-boat crossings, contains a stark message: “To the migrants who come to the UK illegally: you will face deportation or removal.” Most businesses raided by Ice don’t know why they were targeted. Anonymous tipoffs, which can be made online, appear to be behind many of the raids (including the one on Mandira’s Kitchen). Kevin Barker, a former Ice officer and the director of the paralegal firm Immigration Compliance Ltd, says that while raids are always “intelligence-led”, a tipoff can be enough to trigger one. There are steps between a tipoff being filed and a raid being carried out, Barker says. Surveillance is sometimes used, including “discreet drive-by surveillance”. Barker says Ice will also “see if there’s any other allegations or previous immigration raids to the business” and investigate if a name is given. But more often than not, he says, “there are no names mentioned – they just have a suspicion of illegal workers”. The nature of anonymity means a tipoff can relate to a personal or commercial grievance. Often, it’s “competitors within the local area” filling out the forms, he says. Moitra Sarkar holds a sponsor licence, which allows her to employ students or workers who might not normally have the right to work in the UK. “It was all very intimidating. Oh, you’re brown and you’re running a food business? Of course you’ve got illegal immigrants. It’s guilty until proven innocent,” she says. Racial profiling is a factor, says Seema Syeda, the advocacy and communications director at the Joint Council for the Welfare of Immigrants, which provides legal advice for those targeted by raids. She says the high number of arrests doesn’t paint the full picture. In 2025, Ice officers carried out 12,791 illegal-working raids and made 8,971 arrests. However, the Home Office’s figures show that only a quarter of those arrested (2,251) were detained and 12% (1,087) left the UK, either by force or voluntarily. Syeda says she has seen “many cases where people are raided, arrested and then released”. It is, she says, “very clear to us that this is a performative act”. *** When I arrive at Facing Heaven, the owner, Julian Denis, looks hesitant. I’m wearing all black and, for a moment, he worries Ice has returned. His business, a trendy vegan Chinese restaurant near Broadway Market in east London, was raided in September. It was Saturday night and the restaurant, decorated in bright pink and neon, was packed. About 10 officers made a beeline for the open-plan kitchen. They separated the staff before interrogating them individually, asking to see their IDs and payslips. Apart from speaking to the restaurant’s general manager, James Nolan, the officers didn’t speak to any white members of staff that night. They “singled out non-white, foreign-born workers for interrogation, scaring them with the possibility of being immediately jailed”, says Nolan. The lead officer told Nolan they were there off the back of an anonymous tipoff that specified “illegal Bengali workers”. After failing to find any illegal workers in the kitchen, having carried out their interrogations in plain sight of diners, the officers asked Nolan if there were any more members of staff on the premises. He said there weren’t. He was then asked the question again, replying with the same answer. Upon being asked a third time, Nolan got frustrated and said not “unless they’re on the fucking roof”. At this point, the officer “gave up trying to catch me out”, he says. The officer told Nolan that they were looking for “five to six illegal workers”. After about half an hour, with the dinner service halted, Ice left. Nolan asked an officer why they came during their busiest period, allowing customers to “look at this charade unfold”. The officer said they had to come at a “busy time when all staff were working”. Denis is from the US, where Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has instilled fear in communities of colour by rounding up and detaining people – many of them legal residents or even US citizens – using aggressive and violent tactics. conversation.question: How do the interrogation tactics used by immigration officers in March 2026 compare across the restaurant raid and the shop owner's arrest?
conversation.answer: In the March 24, 2026, restaurant raid, officers used repeated questioning to try to catch the owner out and justify their presence based on an anonymous tipoff about 'illegal Bengali workers' during a busy service. In the March 6, 2026, shop owner incident, officers used physical force, including kneeling on the subject's back and handcuffing her without showing a warrant, while actively obscuring documentation efforts by the owner's daughter and threatening her with arrest for 'obstruction'.
conversation.rag_answer:

**UK Restaurant Raids (by Home Office Ice officers):**
