Title: ‘They singled out non-white, foreign-born workers’: the restaurants raided by Britain’s version of ICE 
Date: 2026-03-24

Diners were tucking into their upmarket Indian lunch when the Ice agents slid through the restaurant’s back fence. Clad in stab vests, the 11-strong unit blocked off every entrance before moving in on their target: Mandira’s Kitchen. This wasn’t a scene from California or Texas. It happened near Guildford, England, among the rolling Surrey Hills. Before the Home Office’s immigration compliance and enforcement (Ice) officers stormed the restaurant in September, they came up with a codeword in the event they were attacked with any weapons that might be at hand in a kitchen. What they found were customers eating biryani and samosas in a converted barn decorated with plants and a rickshaw bicycle hanging from the ceiling. When they reached the kitchen, they found five junior members of staff cooking. The officers demanded to see their passports. “They didn’t explain. They didn’t ask for permission,” says the restaurant’s owner, Mandira Moitra Sarkar. That 11 officers could burst into her business with no warrant and question staff is “astounding”, she says. Moitra Sarkar was on holiday in Tanzania when Ice arrived; she was notified by a frantic call from a member of staff. Moitra Sarkar made her name in the restaurant industry after being lauded by culinary giants including Nigella Lawson and Jamie Oliver. Her prepared dishes, which can be bought online for home delivery, were featured on a Channel 4 competition to find the country’s best ready meals for Aldi. But this wasn’t enough for the Home Office. Its crackdown on illegal working has left many business owners and employees reeling from its heavy-handed tactics. The immigration officers – from South Central Ice, which covers a patchwork of southern England including Portsmouth, Plymouth and Surrey – were accompanied by two officers from Surrey police, who walked around the restaurant among the customers, watching the scene unfold, and then blocked entry to the toilets. Once the Ice officers entered the kitchen, the members of staff were separated and questioned individually. One employee was asked whether his name was really Shamriaz (officers insisted it was Shabbir); another was quizzed on his wife and his child; one was asked about their studies after showing a university ID. Moitra Sarkar says the officers asked questions such as: “When was the last time you went to university? What grades do you get? Do you get your assignments done on time?” She believes it was “completely ad hoc”. After 37 gruelling minutes, having failed to find any wrongdoing, the Ice officers left the premises. To top it all off, Moitra Sarkar says, the Home Office vans left the restaurant car park without paying – non-customers are usually charged £2. The raid is one of more than 17,400 on businesses carried out since Labour came to power in July 2024 – a 77% increase on the year before and almost as many as in the entire previous parliament. The government says the drive has led to the highest number of arrests for illegal working since records began, but critics say the crackdown goes far beyond the party’s manifesto pledge of a “fair and properly managed immigration system”. While the UK’s Ice teams were set up in 2013 as part of Theresa May’s “hostile environment” policy, Labour has been using the increased number of raids to front much of the Home Office’s media output. In January, the Home Office set up a TikTok account, @SecureBordersUK, to show Ice officers raiding a market, a car wash and a nail bar. One video, which appears to show people struggling in open water during small-boat crossings, contains a stark message: “To the migrants who come to the UK illegally: you will face deportation or removal.” Most businesses raided by Ice don’t know why they were targeted. Anonymous tipoffs, which can be made online, appear to be behind many of the raids (including the one on Mandira’s Kitchen). Kevin Barker, a former Ice officer and the director of the paralegal firm Immigration Compliance Ltd, says that while raids are always “intelligence-led”, a tipoff can be enough to trigger one. There are steps between a tipoff being filed and a raid being carried out, Barker says. Surveillance is sometimes used, including “discreet drive-by surveillance”. Barker says Ice will also “see if there’s any other allegations or previous immigration raids to the business” and investigate if a name is given. But more often than not, he says, “there are no names mentioned – they just have a suspicion of illegal workers”. The nature of anonymity means a tipoff can relate to a personal or commercial grievance. Often, it’s “competitors within the local area” filling out the forms, he says. Moitra Sarkar holds a sponsor licence, which allows her to employ students or workers who might not normally have the right to work in the UK. “It was all very intimidating. Oh, you’re brown and you’re running a food business? Of course you’ve got illegal immigrants. It’s guilty until proven innocent,” she says. Racial profiling is a factor, says Seema Syeda, the advocacy and communications director at the Joint Council for the Welfare of Immigrants, which provides legal advice for those targeted by raids. She says the high number of arrests doesn’t paint the full picture. In 2025, Ice officers carried out 12,791 illegal-working raids and made 8,971 arrests. However, the Home Office’s figures show that only a quarter of those arrested (2,251) were detained and 12% (1,087) left the UK, either by force or voluntarily. Syeda says she has seen “many cases where people are raided, arrested and then released”. It is, she says, “very clear to us that this is a performative act”. *** When I arrive at Facing Heaven, the owner, Julian Denis, looks hesitant. I’m wearing all black and, for a moment, he worries Ice has returned. His business, a trendy vegan Chinese restaurant near Broadway Market in east London, was raided in September. It was Saturday night and the restaurant, decorated in bright pink and neon, was packed. About 10 officers made a beeline for the open-plan kitchen. They separated the staff before interrogating them individually, asking to see their IDs and payslips. Apart from speaking to the restaurant’s general manager, James Nolan, the officers didn’t speak to any white members of staff that night. They “singled out non-white, foreign-born workers for interrogation, scaring them with the possibility of being immediately jailed”, says Nolan. The lead officer told Nolan they were there off the back of an anonymous tipoff that specified “illegal Bengali workers”. After failing to find any illegal workers in the kitchen, having carried out their interrogations in plain sight of diners, the officers asked Nolan if there were any more members of staff on the premises. He said there weren’t. He was then asked the question again, replying with the same answer. Upon being asked a third time, Nolan got frustrated and said not “unless they’re on the fucking roof”. At this point, the officer “gave up trying to catch me out”, he says. The officer told Nolan that they were looking for “five to six illegal workers”. After about half an hour, with the dinner service halted, Ice left. Nolan asked an officer why they came during their busiest period, allowing customers to “look at this charade unfold”. The officer said they had to come at a “busy time when all staff were working”. Denis is from the US, where Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has instilled fear in communities of colour by rounding up and detaining people – many of them legal residents or even US citizens – using aggressive and violent tactics. This feeling has been intensified by the filmed killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti by federal agents. Denis describes the raid on his restaurant as “a wake-up call”. He says his employees, who are “legally employed and have the full right to work in the UK, should not have to come to work feeling fearful because of their country of origin”. Denis has a two-word message for the Home Office: “Fuck Ice.” Mandira’s Kitchen and Facing Heaven were raided under the Licensing Act 2003, which makes restaurants particularly vulnerable to surprise visits. It allows Ice officers to enter a business when it is carrying out a licensed activity, such as serving alcohol or selling hot food or drinks after 11pm, without a warrant. In 2025, 28% of all raids were carried out on restaurants, takeaways and cafes, a proportion significantly higher than for any other type of business. Barker claims that officers often go beyond the powers of the act, which allows them only to enter a business. Once inside, they treat it as though they have “carte blanche to speak to whoever they want to and search wherever they want to”. For example, Ice could enter a restaurant on the basis of concerns over someone without the right to work selling alcohol, but end up rounding up kitchen staff or delivery drivers, who “have no part in the sale of alcohol”. Ice was given greater power to enter businesses in 2017 after amendments to the Licensing Act were passed. The changes had some notable critics, including the MP for Holborn and St Pancras, Keir Starmer, who told the Commons in 2015 that they would give officers a “much lower threshold than the usual threshold for entering premises”. Barker says the amended act has proved “very powerful” for Ice: “Quite often, I would argue that their intentions have not been anything to do with the licensed activity. It’s just used to give them the power to enter.” In Surrey, Moitra Sarkar is still seeking answers. She sent freedom of information requests to the Home Office and Surrey police to find out why her business had been raided. The manager on shift at the time had overheard officers say they had received a tipoff, which has since been confirmed by documents she has obtained. These documents also reveal that the Home Office contacted the council to ask whether it had “licensing concerns” (it didn’t) and invited council officers to join the raid, which Moitra Sarkar believes was an attempt to beef up the legal justification. The “pre-visit report” lays out how Ice used the Licensing Act to authorise its raid without a warrant. It cites the cafe’s website, showing a menu including alcoholic drinks, as evidence that a licensed activity was taking place, which, combined with the tipoff, gave officers the power to enter. Officers are supposed to get “fully informed consent” from the business owner, which involves clearly saying why they are there and what they plan to do, as well as the potential civil or criminal penalties that could result from their visit. Businesses should also be told that they can refuse Ice officers entry and can withdraw their consent at any time. However, when Ice raided Mandira’s Kitchen, Moitra Sarkar says, the general manager asked the officer whether they could come back another day. The Surrey police officer told her: “No, that’s not how it works,” and pressed ahead anyway. When the Guardian asked about the raid, Surrey police said Ice and officers from its modern slavery and organised immigration crime unit carried out a joint visit “to complete a routine licensing check under section 179 of the Licensing Act 2003”. Fizza Qureshi, the CEO of the charity Migrants’ Rights Network (MRN), has spoken to a number of affected business owners, many of whom are from migrant backgrounds. She says many are “not fully informed of what their rights are” and have become resigned to raids being “part of their daily life”. The Home Office doesn’t record ethnicity data when carrying out raids or arrests (although it does record nationality), but research from MRN suggests they are carried out in areas that have large black and Asian populations. Qureshi has spoken to a car wash owner who says he is raided every three months or so, despite no illegal workers being found. The impact can be profound, she says, with raided businesses struggling to shake off the stigma. A Home Office spokesperson said: “All enforcement operations are intelligence-led. Race and ethnicity play no role in operational decisions. Illegal working undermines honest employers, undercuts local wages and fuels organised immigration crime.” “We hold our officers to the highest standards of professionalism and categorically refute any suggestion they acted improperly [or] were heavy‑handed,” the spokesperson added. Tanya Goldfarb, the head of business immigration at the law firm Bindman’s, says care homes, IT companies and hotels have also been targeted by Ice recently. Goldfarb says the raids “are frightening and intimidating” and that people may be handcuffed: “They are taken to detention centres. Sometimes they’re released and sometimes they’re not released. People often don’t know where their loved ones are. They don’t have a chance to make a phone call to anybody.” While Ice has far fewer powers than ICE in the US, this may not remain the case. Reform UK and the Conservatives have announced plans to create agencies modelled on ICE, should they win power. However, the increase in raids hasn’t come without opposition on the ground. Anti-raid groups are “springing up”, says Syeda. In January, a group in Lewisham, south London, alerted people to a car wash raid. When protesters told those being approached by Ice officers of their rights, no one was arrested. For Moitra Sarkar, the raid came during a particularly tough time. Days earlier, more than 100,000 people had marched through central London for “unite the kingdom”, a far-right rally at which racist conspiracy theories and anti-Muslim hate speech rang through Whitehall. Her daughter, who was born and raised in Surrey, was told not to go to work that week. “I’ve lived in Britain for 28 years and I’m the most optimistic person that there is,” says Moitra Sarkar. “But so many things happened that week.” Aside from the personal impact of the raid, Moitra Sarkar thinks it was a waste of officers’ time and taxpayers’ money. “We really need to call it out for what it is,” she says. “Completely ludicrous.” • Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in our letters section, please click here. • This article was amended on 24 March 2026. An earlier version referred to Ice officers being armed with stun guns during a raid; this was based on information from a witness account that was put to the Home Office prior to publication. However, the Home Office has since informed us that Ice officers do not possess such devices. An additional comment from the Home Office was added on 25 March 2026.

Title: ICE arrested an Oregon shop owner who had her green card in her pocket: ‘They didn’t care’
Date: 2026-03-06

The moment 19-year-old Emely Agustin spotted the group of masked immigration agents tackling a woman facedown on the pavement, her heart dropped. She saw the woman’s red jacket. “Oh my fucking God. That’s my mom!” she screamed. It was the morning of 5 November in Cottage Grove, in western Oregon, and masked officers with Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) had dragged Juanita Avila out of her van. The agents pinned her down as she cried out for her daughter, who started filming. Juanita, a 47-year-old legal permanent resident, had her green card in her pocket, but officers handcuffed and detained her. “I was really scared. I couldn’t stop shaking,” Emely recalled in a recent interview. “It was like they were trying to kidnap me,” added Juanita. Last month, a federal judge sided with the plaintiffs and issued a preliminary injunction blocking ICE agents in Oregon from continuing their “arrest first, justify later” tactics, as lawyers labeled it. The judge said ICE’s conduct had been “brutal and violent”, saying agents’ arrest strategy “systematically violates federal law and individual constitutional rights”. From ‘village girl’ to shop owner Standing behind the counter at Juanita’s Latina Store one recent morning, Juanita fidgeted with papers and spoke softly about her arrest as customers wandered in for snacks. Her tiny shop, located in a former bank vault, is on Main Street in Cottage Grove, a town south of Eugene. Juanita packs the shelves with an array of goods, including Latin American spices, corn masa flour, breads, cowboy hats and candles. Under a banner of Guatemalan flags, her window advertises money transfer services for immigrants to send funds to relatives. Juanita was born in 1978 in Guatemala. Her father was killed in war when she was three years old, and she immigrated to Los Angeles at age 14. In LA, she first lived with a family that exploited her and forced her to work, she said: “I was a village girl who was so innocent. I still have nightmares.” She later worked at a daycare near the University of California, Los Angeles, that served international families. The owner, an Iranian woman, helped take care of Juanita’s newborn son, and she grew to love Iranian festivities and food. Juanita moved to Cottage Grove with her three children in 2014. She felt safer and more at home in the “quiet and peace” of rural Oregon. She did farmwork, pruning blueberries and foraging for mushrooms. She was grateful for the fresh air, though the hours could be grueling. In 2016, the former owner of the local shop asked Juanita to take over the store. By then, she was known for helping local families with translations, with neighbors regularly asking her to interpret documents. “I didn’t want people coming into my house,” she says, with a laugh. “So this was perfect. I could have a business where I’d be helping the community.” Juanita, who got her green card in 2017, also began helping local police with interpretation. In Donald Trump’s first term, she started assisting neighbors facing deportation. One time, she recalled, she was kicked out of a federal building while trying to interpret for immigrants at their ICE check-ins: “They wanted any excuse for them to get deported. It was horrible.” She was haunted by the memory of an inconsolable three-year-old girl whose mother was detained. But as Trump returned to office, she continued to help immigrant neighbors, a practice that led her into ICE’s crosshairs. ‘I couldn’t breathe’ Around 5.30am on 5 November, Juanita awoke to a call from a friend, saying ICE had arrested a relative whose car was left behind, asking Juanita to retrieve the vehicle so it wouldn’t get towed. Juanita woke up her daughter, and the two drove in separate cars to search for the vehicle. They couldn’t find the car, and as Juanita was returning home, she spotted flashing lights behind her. She pulled over near a motel, assuming she was getting a traffic ticket from police. The men who approached drove unmarked cars, lacked visible badges, asked where she was born and said she was under arrest, she later wrote in a declaration filed as part of the lawsuit. When she responded asking why she was stopped, an officer did not show a warrant or give a reason, her declaration said. The officers reached through her window, opened the door, pulled her out and kneeled on her back, the declaration said. They also accused her of being “illegal”, she wrote. One witness video captured Juanita facedown yelling through sobs: “Why are you arresting me? You are not police … Emely! Emely!” Emely, her US citizen daughter, soon arrived. Footage Emely recorded of the incident shows she immediately explained that her mom had a green card and was carrying it on her. When she yelled to her mom that she was recording, an officer shined a bright light in her face, obscuring her camera’s view. “You don’t need to tackle her. There’s no reason to fucking do this,” Emely pleaded. The officers handcuffed Juanita and put her in their car. One officer then told Emely she could also be arrested for “obstruction”, witness video shows. The officers eventually uncuffed Juanita so she could grab her green card. When she again asked why they had arrested her and didn’t believe her, an agent responded: “Because they lie all the time,” according to Emely’s footage. Juanita was released. The encounter did not lead to charges and lasted roughly 30 minutes. One of the scariest moments, Juanita recalled, was being thrown into ICE’s vehicle “like a log”, leaving her weeping and coughing through a panic attack: “I couldn’t breathe.” While under arrest, she thought of her 12-year-old son with autism, fearing what would happen to him if she were jailed: “I said: ‘My son is at home. Don’t take me. Don’t do this.’ They didn’t listen. They didn’t care.” Juanita said she was terrified for her daughter, but added: “If Emely wasn’t there, they would have taken me.” Emely, who is studying to teach English as a second language, said after the agent shined the light in her face, she fell off a curb and badly injured her knee: “My voice was trembling and I thought if I was arrested, who is going to help my mom? … All I wanted to do was hug her.” Juanita feared agents had broken her shoulder and went to the hospital. She then visited the police station to report the incident. Forced into hiding Juanita’s arrest stunned Cottage Grove residents. “A lot of people did not believe ICE was doing these things until they saw what happened to Juanita,” said Abelio Carrillo Chales, an organizer with the Rural Organizing Project, a non-profit headquartered in town. “People were shocked and horrified, because she is well-known and has been here for so long. People were really concerned about the safety of their neighbors.” Locals organized a vigil to support Juanita and others affected by ICE and began doing early morning patrols and joining rapid response networks, Carrillo said. DHS later confirmed ICE had detained nine people in Cottage Grove that day. Three of those arrested were Carrillo’s family members, he said. One has since been deported to Guatemala; another was released and a third remains detained. The November operation, Carrillo said, sent many immigrants into hiding, regardless of their status. Children stayed home from school. Parents skipped work. Families struggled to keep up with necessities, like groceries. Carrillo, who was on the scene for Juanita’s arrest, said he was shaken by the episode and had to take time off work. He felt anguish over his inability to intervene, but knew if he got any closer, he risked detention: “I was really in fear for my life.” Emely said she had to go on medications for depression and PTSD and found herself shaking anytime a suspicious car drove by. As Juanita’s bruises faded, she became overwhelmed by the community support. She had spent her whole life helping others and didn’t know how to respond to residents leaving notes and flowers at the store and offering assistance, she said. For more than a month, her older son ran the shop while she largely stayed in bed. “My therapist said, you better get up. Go back to work. Show them you’re not defeated. The community needs you,” she said. Juanita soon was helping others affected by ICE’s crackdown, bringing food to a young girl who had stopped eating after her father was detained and picking up farmworkers who had been hiding from ICE in the woods. And she joined the lawsuit. “I was scared, because it’s the government. Who is going to win against the government?” she said. It was painful to keep retelling her account, she added: “But I told Emely, we have to tell our story. Our voices have to be heard.” ICE quotas and an app In January, Juanita’s arrest became part of a class-action suit filed by the Innovation Law Lab, an Oregon-based immigrants’ rights non-profit. Without warrants, the attorneys said, ICE can only arrest people if there is probable cause that the person is in the country without authorization and “likely to escape before a warrant can be obtained”. Instead, ICE was consistently violating people’s due-process rights, often stopping them based on their skin color, with no justification. The suit exposed details of how ICE had targeted people without warrants, with officers testifying about the Department of Homeland Security’s “Operation Black Rose”, which launched in Portland last fall and, according to DHS, led to more than 1,200 arrests through mid-December. One ICE officer testified that Black Rose teams of roughly nine to 12 officers were told to target eight arrests per day, with plaintiff’s attorneys estimating this amounted to a daily quota of 50 arrests across Oregon. The officer also revealed agents chose locations with help from a DHS app called “Elite”, which identified target areas where there might be multiple people with some “nexus” to immigration (including documented immigrants without violations). “It’s a dragnet of arresting whoever, wherever just because they look a certain way or work in a certain industry or don’t speak English,” said Nelly Garcia Orjuela, Innovation Law Lab staff attorney. Victor Cruz Gamez, the leading named plaintiff, has lived in the US for 25 years and has a work permit and protection against deportation. The father of three was nonetheless arrested by ICE in October when agents mixed him up with a different Victor Cruz, and he spent three weeks in detention before he was released. In an interview, he said he wanted to protect others and show that the government was not targeting “criminals and rapists”, as DHS has claimed. “They just see us as numbers. They don’t see us as human beings,” he said. ‘There is hope’ On 4 February, the US judge Mustafa Kasubhai sided with Innovation Law Lab, broadly halting ICE’s warrantless arrests in Oregon, saying agents could only detain people without warrants if the agents produced individualized narratives documenting the probable cause they had pre-arrest to carry out the detention. “There can be no injury more irreparable than being illegally arrested in the manner in which [plaintiffs were] stopped, detained, thrown to the ground and handcuffed,” said the judge, who said Juanita’s arrest was “without any justification”. Oregon is only the third jurisdiction with these protections, which federal judges ordered in similar cases in Colorado and Washington DC. Cruz said the ruling meant people could feel comfortable leaving their homes for the first time to get groceries or go to the doctor: “I am so relieved … There is still hope.” It’s unclear whether officers have complied with the ruling, though attorneys said there were anecdotal reports of reduced ICE activity. In Colorado, ICE has been accused of violating the federal order blocking warrantless arrests. DHS and the US justice department did not respond to inquiries about the ruling or Juanita’s arrest. Thomas Ratcliffe, the US attorney, had argued Juanita’s arrest should be excluded from the case since it was an “investigative detention”, not an arrest, but the judge rejected that argument. Ratcliffe in court also apologized to Cruz for what he had endured and expressed remorse to Emely after her testimony. He acknowledged her mother’s arrest had interfered with her final exams, saying: “I hope that you, your brother and your whole family can put this behind you.” For her part, Emely said the ruling offered some solace, though she questioned whether officers would follow it, saying: “It’s the only way my family is going to get justice.” Her family, she said, was slowly moving forward, but she has noticed changes in her mother, who was once outgoing, but is now reserved. Her mom often sits in her store, watching in fear as vehicles pass by. Emely, who recently turned 20, also said her 12-year-old brother became afraid to go to school, began lashing out and started obsessively watching ICE videos, which caused further distress. He is now in therapy, she said, and she has made sure that he never saw the footage she took of their mom’s arrest.

conversation.rag_input: That sounds incredibly invasive. Did the owners have any legal recourse to challenge this behavior after the officers left?
conversation.question: Did the owners of Mandira's Kitchen and Facing Heaven have any legal recourse or ability to challenge the Home Office immigration officers' conduct after the raids in March 2026?
conversation.answer: Following the raids, the business owners often faced significant challenges in seeking legal recourse. While they could theoretically lodge formal complaints with the Home Office or seek judicial reviews regarding the lawfulness of the entry and the conduct of the officers, many owners reported that the immediate impact—such as business disruption, reputational damage, and the loss of staff—made formal legal action difficult. In some instances, owners consulted with legal professionals to determine if the officers had exceeded their authority under the Licensing Act 2003, specifically questioning whether the 'entry for licensed activities' justification was used as a pretext for general immigration enforcement.
conversation.rag_answer:
Yes, the owners did have legal recourse to challenge the behavior of immigration officers after the raids. 

Mandira Moitra Sarkar, owner of Mandira’s Kitchen, actively sought answers by sending **Freedom of Information requests** to the Home Office and Surrey police to ascertain the justification for the raid on her business. Through these requests, she obtained documents confirming that the raid was based on an anonymous tip-off and that the Home Office had contacted the council regarding licensing concerns (though the council had none), suggesting an attempt to bolster legal justification. This demonstrates a direct challenge to the transparency and legal basis of the raid.

The article also indicates the availability of broader legal support. Organizations such as the **Joint Council for the Welfare of Immigrants (JCWI)** provide legal advice for those targeted by raids, and paralegal firms like **Immigration Compliance Ltd** and law firms such as **Bindman’s** (specializing in business immigration) also exist to assist businesses in navigating these situations. These entities highlight that avenues for legal challenge, advice, and support are available to affected business owners. 

While Mandira Moitra Sarkar pursued information requests, the document does not specify any particular legal actions taken by Julian Denis, owner of Facing Heaven, after the raid on his restaurant.

Title: ‘They singled out non-white, foreign-born workers’: the restaurants raided by Britain’s version of ICE 
Date: 2026-03-24

Diners were tucking into their upmarket Indian lunch when the Ice agents slid through the restaurant’s back fence. Clad in stab vests, the 11-strong unit blocked off every entrance before moving in on their target: Mandira’s Kitchen. This wasn’t a scene from California or Texas. It happened near Guildford, England, among the rolling Surrey Hills. Before the Home Office’s immigration compliance and enforcement (Ice) officers stormed the restaurant in September, they came up with a codeword in the event they were attacked with any weapons that might be at hand in a kitchen. What they found were customers eating biryani and samosas in a converted barn decorated with plants and a rickshaw bicycle hanging from the ceiling. When they reached the kitchen, they found five junior members of staff cooking. The officers demanded to see their passports. “They didn’t explain. They didn’t ask for permission,” says the restaurant’s owner, Mandira Moitra Sarkar. That 11 officers could burst into her business with no warrant and question staff is “astounding”, she says. Moitra Sarkar was on holiday in Tanzania when Ice arrived; she was notified by a frantic call from a member of staff. Moitra Sarkar made her name in the restaurant industry after being lauded by culinary giants including Nigella Lawson and Jamie Oliver. Her prepared dishes, which can be bought online for home delivery, were featured on a Channel 4 competition to find the country’s best ready meals for Aldi. But this wasn’t enough for the Home Office. Its crackdown on illegal working has left many business owners and employees reeling from its heavy-handed tactics. The immigration officers – from South Central Ice, which covers a patchwork of southern England including Portsmouth, Plymouth and Surrey – were accompanied by two officers from Surrey police, who walked around the restaurant among the customers, watching the scene unfold, and then blocked entry to the toilets. Once the Ice officers entered the kitchen, the members of staff were separated and questioned individually. One employee was asked whether his name was really Shamriaz (officers insisted it was Shabbir); another was quizzed on his wife and his child; one was asked about their studies after showing a university ID. Moitra Sarkar says the officers asked questions such as: “When was the last time you went to university? What grades do you get? Do you get your assignments done on time?” She believes it was “completely ad hoc”. After 37 gruelling minutes, having failed to find any wrongdoing, the Ice officers left the premises. To top it all off, Moitra Sarkar says, the Home Office vans left the restaurant car park without paying – non-customers are usually charged £2. The raid is one of more than 17,400 on businesses carried out since Labour came to power in July 2024 – a 77% increase on the year before and almost as many as in the entire previous parliament. The government says the drive has led to the highest number of arrests for illegal working since records began, but critics say the crackdown goes far beyond the party’s manifesto pledge of a “fair and properly managed immigration system”. While the UK’s Ice teams were set up in 2013 as part of Theresa May’s “hostile environment” policy, Labour has been using the increased number of raids to front much of the Home Office’s media output. In January, the Home Office set up a TikTok account, @SecureBordersUK, to show Ice officers raiding a market, a car wash and a nail bar. One video, which appears to show people struggling in open water during small-boat crossings, contains a stark message: “To the migrants who come to the UK illegally: you will face deportation or removal.” Most businesses raided by Ice don’t know why they were targeted. Anonymous tipoffs, which can be made online, appear to be behind many of the raids (including the one on Mandira’s Kitchen). Kevin Barker, a former Ice officer and the director of the paralegal firm Immigration Compliance Ltd, says that while raids are always “intelligence-led”, a tipoff can be enough to trigger one. There are steps between a tipoff being filed and a raid being carried out, Barker says. Surveillance is sometimes used, including “discreet drive-by surveillance”. Barker says Ice will also “see if there’s any other allegations or previous immigration raids to the business” and investigate if a name is given. But more often than not, he says, “there are no names mentioned – they just have a suspicion of illegal workers”. The nature of anonymity means a tipoff can relate to a personal or commercial grievance. Often, it’s “competitors within the local area” filling out the forms, he says. Moitra Sarkar holds a sponsor licence, which allows her to employ students or workers who might not normally have the right to work in the UK. “It was all very intimidating. Oh, you’re brown and you’re running a food business? Of course you’ve got illegal immigrants. It’s guilty until proven innocent,” she says. Racial profiling is a factor, says Seema Syeda, the advocacy and communications director at the Joint Council for the Welfare of Immigrants, which provides legal advice for those targeted by raids. She says the high number of arrests doesn’t paint the full picture. In 2025, Ice officers carried out 12,791 illegal-working raids and made 8,971 arrests. However, the Home Office’s figures show that only a quarter of those arrested (2,251) were detained and 12% (1,087) left the UK, either by force or voluntarily. Syeda says she has seen “many cases where people are raided, arrested and then released”. It is, she says, “very clear to us that this is a performative act”. *** When I arrive at Facing Heaven, the owner, Julian Denis, looks hesitant. I’m wearing all black and, for a moment, he worries Ice has returned. His business, a trendy vegan Chinese restaurant near Broadway Market in east London, was raided in September. It was Saturday night and the restaurant, decorated in bright pink and neon, was packed. About 10 officers made a beeline for the open-plan kitchen. They separated the staff before interrogating them individually, asking to see their IDs and payslips. Apart from speaking to the restaurant’s general manager, James Nolan, the officers didn’t speak to any white members of staff that night. They “singled out non-white, foreign-born workers for interrogation, scaring them with the possibility of being immediately jailed”, says Nolan. The lead officer told Nolan they were there off the back of an anonymous tipoff that specified “illegal Bengali workers”. After failing to find any illegal workers in the kitchen, having carried out their interrogations in plain sight of diners, the officers asked Nolan if there were any more members of staff on the premises. He said there weren’t. He was then asked the question again, replying with the same answer. Upon being asked a third time, Nolan got frustrated and said not “unless they’re on the fucking roof”. At this point, the officer “gave up trying to catch me out”, he says. The officer told Nolan that they were looking for “five to six illegal workers”. After about half an hour, with the dinner service halted, Ice left. Nolan asked an officer why they came during their busiest period, allowing customers to “look at this charade unfold”. The officer said they had to come at a “busy time when all staff were working”. Denis is from the US, where Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has instilled fear in communities of colour by rounding up and detaining people – many of them legal residents or even US citizens – using aggressive and violent tactics. This feeling has been intensified by the filmed killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti by federal agents. Denis describes the raid on his restaurant as “a wake-up call”. He says his employees, who are “legally employed and have the full right to work in the UK, should not have to come to work feeling fearful because of their country of origin”. Denis has a two-word message for the Home Office: “Fuck Ice.” Mandira’s Kitchen and Facing Heaven were raided under the Licensing Act 2003, which makes restaurants particularly vulnerable to surprise visits. It allows Ice officers to enter a business when it is carrying out a licensed activity, such as serving alcohol or selling hot food or drinks after 11pm, without a warrant. In 2025, 28% of all raids were carried out on restaurants, takeaways and cafes, a proportion significantly higher than for any other type of business. Barker claims that officers often go beyond the powers of the act, which allows them only to enter a business. Once inside, they treat it as though they have “carte blanche to speak to whoever they want to and search wherever they want to”. For example, Ice could enter a restaurant on the basis of concerns over someone without the right to work selling alcohol, but end up rounding up kitchen staff or delivery drivers, who “have no part in the sale of alcohol”. Ice was given greater power to enter businesses in 2017 after amendments to the Licensing Act were passed. The changes had some notable critics, including the MP for Holborn and St Pancras, Keir Starmer, who told the Commons in 2015 that they would give officers a “much lower threshold than the usual threshold for entering premises”. Barker says the amended act has proved “very powerful” for Ice: “Quite often, I would argue that their intentions have not been anything to do with the licensed activity. It’s just used to give them the power to enter.” In Surrey, Moitra Sarkar is still seeking answers. She sent freedom of information requests to the Home Office and Surrey police to find out why her business had been raided. The manager on shift at the time had overheard officers say they had received a tipoff, which has since been confirmed by documents she has obtained. These documents also reveal that the Home Office contacted the council to ask whether it had “licensing concerns” (it didn’t) and invited council officers to join the raid, which Moitra Sarkar believes was an attempt to beef up the legal justification. The “pre-visit report” lays out how Ice used the Licensing Act to authorise its raid without a warrant. It cites the cafe’s website, showing a menu including alcoholic drinks, as evidence that a licensed activity was taking place, which, combined with the tipoff, gave officers the power to enter. Officers are supposed to get “fully informed consent” from the business owner, which involves clearly saying why they are there and what they plan to do, as well as the potential civil or criminal penalties that could result from their visit. Businesses should also be told that they can refuse Ice officers entry and can withdraw their consent at any time. However, when Ice raided Mandira’s Kitchen, Moitra Sarkar says, the general manager asked the officer whether they could come back another day. The Surrey police officer told her: “No, that’s not how it works,” and pressed ahead anyway. When the Guardian asked about the raid, Surrey police said Ice and officers from its modern slavery and organised immigration crime unit carried out a joint visit “to complete a routine licensing check under section 179 of the Licensing Act 2003”. Fizza Qureshi, the CEO of the charity Migrants’ Rights Network (MRN), has spoken to a number of affected business owners, many of whom are from migrant backgrounds. She says many are “not fully informed of what their rights are” and have become resigned to raids being “part of their daily life”. The Home Office doesn’t record ethnicity data when carrying out raids or arrests (although it does record nationality), but research from MRN suggests they are carried out in areas that have large black and Asian populations. Qureshi has spoken to a car wash owner who says he is raided every three months or so, despite no illegal workers being found. The impact can be profound, she says, with raided businesses struggling to shake off the stigma. A Home Office spokesperson said: “All enforcement operations are intelligence-led. Race and ethnicity play no role in operational decisions. Illegal working undermines honest employers, undercuts local wages and fuels organised immigration crime.” “We hold our officers to the highest standards of professionalism and categorically refute any suggestion they acted improperly [or] were heavy‑handed,” the spokesperson added. Tanya Goldfarb, the head of business immigration at the law firm Bindman’s, says care homes, IT companies and hotels have also been targeted by Ice recently. Goldfarb says the raids “are frightening and intimidating” and that people may be handcuffed: “They are taken to detention centres. Sometimes they’re released and sometimes they’re not released. People often don’t know where their loved ones are. They don’t have a chance to make a phone call to anybody.” While Ice has far fewer powers than ICE in the US, this may not remain the case. Reform UK and the Conservatives have announced plans to create agencies modelled on ICE, should they win power. However, the increase in raids hasn’t come without opposition on the ground. Anti-raid groups are “springing up”, says Syeda. In January, a group in Lewisham, south London, alerted people to a car wash raid. When protesters told those being approached by Ice officers of their rights, no one was arrested. For Moitra Sarkar, the raid came during a particularly tough time. Days earlier, more than 100,000 people had marched through central London for “unite the kingdom”, a far-right rally at which racist conspiracy theories and anti-Muslim hate speech rang through Whitehall. Her daughter, who was born and raised in Surrey, was told not to go to work that week. “I’ve lived in Britain for 28 years and I’m the most optimistic person that there is,” says Moitra Sarkar. “But so many things happened that week.” Aside from the personal impact of the raid, Moitra Sarkar thinks it was a waste of officers’ time and taxpayers’ money. “We really need to call it out for what it is,” she says. “Completely ludicrous.” • Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in our letters section, please click here. • This article was amended on 24 March 2026. An earlier version referred to Ice officers being armed with stun guns during a raid; this was based on information from a witness account that was put to the Home Office prior to publication. However, the Home Office has since informed us that Ice officers do not possess such devices. An additional comment from the Home Office was added on 25 March 2026.

conversation.rag_input: That makes sense, but how do these raids typically impact the actual employees who are caught in the middle of this, especially if they are just doing their jobs?
conversation.question: How do immigration enforcement raids in restaurants typically impact the employees who are working at the time of the incident?
conversation.answer: The impact on employees during these raids is often profound and traumatic. Beyond the immediate distress of being separated from colleagues and subjected to intense, personal questioning, employees frequently experience significant psychological stress and a sense of humiliation. Those who are non-white or foreign-born report feeling specifically targeted, which can lead to a long-term sense of insecurity and fear of law enforcement. In cases where workers are detained or their employment status is questioned, they face potential loss of livelihood, disruption of their personal lives, and the risk of deportation or legal proceedings, regardless of whether they have a legal right to work. Furthermore, the invasive nature of questioning—demanding details about their personal lives and families—often leaves workers feeling dehumanized and vulnerable.
conversation.rag_answer:
Immigration raids have a significant and often traumatic impact on employees who are caught in the middle, even when simply doing their jobs. These impacts include:

*   **Fear and Anxiety:** Employees experience intense fear, leading some to call out of work or even hide from enforcement officers. Co-workers have been observed crying, and customers also become visibly scared during raids.
*   **Increased Workload:** When co-workers are absent due to fear of ICE, the remaining employees are forced to take on their workload, adding to their stress and duties.
*   **Psychological Distress:** The constant threat and presence of immigration enforcement create a pervasive sense of anxiety among workers. As one worker noted, "The fear is there and if people don’t know their rights, the fear will always be there in their minds."
*   **Disruption of Daily Life:** The fear extends beyond the workplace, affecting employees' willingness to leave their homes due to the proximity of enforcement activities.
*   **Lack of Employer Support:** In some instances, employers fail to affirm support for their employees, with one McDonald's management even advising workers to hide in their cars if ICE arrived, further isolating and frightening them.

To mitigate these impacts, workers and unions advocate for employers to affirm workers' rights, ensure access to private, protected areas in the workplace, and deny entry to officers without a judicial warrant. Education on their rights is seen as crucial to empowering employees and reducing their fear.
context:
Title: California fast food workers, still reeling from ICE raids, demand employers step up
Date: 2026-02-27

Fast food workers in California are demanding employers sign a pledge reaffirming workers’ rights amid Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) raids at workplaces across the US. The California Fast Food Workers Union, an affiliate of the Service Employees International Union, drafted a Constitutional Pledge to California Workers’ Rights for workplaces to sign that affirms a commitment to protecting workers and “keep ICE from going where they are not allowed”. The pledge includes a list of actions that would protect vulnerable workers, such as making sure workers have access to private, protected areas in the workplace and ensuring that enforcement officers aren’t entering these spaces without a judicial warrant. In San Jose, workers at a McDonald’s franchise walked out earlier this month after management declined to affirm they would help employees if ICE showed up, telling workers to instead hide in their cars should enforcement arrive. “McDonald’s has the legal right to deny [immigration enforcement officers] entry to private areas of the store if they don’t present a warrant signed by a federal judge,” wrote the California Fast Food Workers Union in response to the strike, adding that other “businesses have put up signs as well as trained their staff to enforce that right”. Candida Masin, a McDonald’s worker in Los Angeles, told the Guardian that as immigration enforcement ramped up over last summer, co-workers started calling out of work. “They say that they are not going to come to work because ICE is close and they are afraid to leave their home. That impacts us because we have to take on their workload,” Masin said, adding that it has also affected the number of customers visiting her store. “ICE agents were raiding areas just two blocks from where I was and I saw my co-workers crying, I saw customers that were really scared. I had to take on double duty to help calm their nerves because I could see how this fear really impacts people.” Masin said she and other fast food workers have visited dozens of different franchise locations to push them to sign on to and post the pledge, which outlines how workplaces can keep “ICE and other federal agents from going where they are legally not allowed, protect workers’ privacy and ensure workers’ safety”. Though the immigration enforcement operations in Los Angeles have calmed since last summer, ICE continues to conduct raids and arrests across the city, which is home to an estimated 3.5 million immigrants. Of the more than 630,000 fast food workers in California, over a quarter are immigrants. Fast food workers in Los Angeles and Santa Clarita counties in California have also been pushing for a fast food fair work ordinance, which would provide paid trainings for workers to learn about their rights on the job. Masin argued it’s important for employers to educate workers and affirm their rights in the workplace to mitigate the anxiety they have when showing up to work. “The fear is there and if people don’t know their rights, the fear will always be there in their minds,” added Masin. “It helps when you know your rights. If you don’t, then you won’t feel a difference in your workplace.” McDonald’s and the California Restaurant Association did not respond to multiple requests for comment. Donald Trump’s ramped up immigration enforcement in California has resulted in a downturn in private sector jobs throughout the state and economic losses for businesses. Following Trump’s deployment of the national guard to Los Angeles last summer, private sector jobs recorded a 5% decline in June and July, according to a recent study by the University of California Merced’s Community and Labor Center. A Los Angeles county report found that 82% of local businesses reported negative impacts from the immigration raids and 44% reported losses of greater than half their normal revenue. “Working people across Los Angeles are demanding global fast food employers sign the constitutional pledge to declare they are choosing courage over silence and dignity over fear,” said David Green, president and executive director of SEIU Local 721 in Los Angeles. “Fast-food companies must stand with cooks and cashiers in this moment, and do their part in building workplaces and communities where all of us can thrive together, stronger and unafraid.”

conversation.rag_input: That sounds incredibly stressful for everyone involved. Are there any specific legal or organizational protections in place that can actually force an employer to follow those safety protocols, or is it mostly voluntary?
conversation.question: Are there specific legal or organizational protections that mandate employers to implement safety protocols during immigration raids, such as demanding a judicial warrant, or are these practices purely voluntary on the part of the employer?
conversation.answer: Generally, there is no federal law that mandates an employer implement specific internal safety protocols for immigration raids. While the Fourth Amendment protects against unreasonable searches and seizures, requiring a judicial warrant for ICE to enter non-public areas of a business, employers are not legally required by labor or employment law to adopt specific policies like "hiding in cars" or granting employees access to private areas. However, many unions negotiate workplace safety and anti-harassment clauses in collective bargaining agreements that can codify how an employer must respond to enforcement actions. Additionally, certain state laws (like those in California, such as the Immigrant Worker Protection Act) impose specific obligations on employers regarding how they must handle ICE access to employee records or work areas, which can effectively force employers to follow protective procedures.
conversation.rag_answer:
Based on the provided information, the safety protocols for employees during immigration raids appear to be largely voluntary or advocated for, rather than legally enforceable mandates on employers. 

Fast food workers and unions, such as the California Fast Food Workers Union, are actively pushing for employers to sign a "Constitutional Pledge to California Workers’ Rights." This pledge outlines actions like ensuring workers have access to private, protected areas and that enforcement officers do not enter without a judicial warrant. However, the fact that employers are asked to "sign" a pledge, and that some, like a McDonald's franchise in San Jose, have "declined to affirm" support, indicates that adherence is not compulsory.

While employers are noted to have the "legal right to deny [immigration enforcement officers] entry to private areas of the store if they don’t present a warrant signed by a federal judge," the document does not state that employers are *forced* to exercise this right as a mandated safety protocol for their employees. The push for a "fast food fair work ordinance" that *would* provide paid trainings for workers to learn about their rights also suggests that such comprehensive, mandated protections are still being sought rather than already being in place.

conversation.rag_input: If those protections are mostly voluntary, how do those proposed ordinances differ from the voluntary pledges that some franchises are refusing to sign?
conversation.question: What are the functional differences between voluntary employer pledges regarding worker rights during immigration raids and the proposed fast food fair work ordinances?
conversation.answer: The primary difference lies in the nature of enforcement and legal obligation. Voluntary pledges are essentially non-binding moral or public relations commitments; if an employer refuses to sign or fails to uphold them, there are no legal repercussions. Conversely, a fair work ordinance functions as a local law or regulation passed by a municipal government. Once enacted, it creates mandatory legal requirements that employers must follow under penalty of law (such as fines, audits, or civil liability). While a pledge relies on the employer’s willingness to comply, an ordinance mandates specific conduct, such as providing paid training on rights or requiring managers to follow specific legal protocols when interacting with immigration officers.
conversation.rag_answer:
The fundamental difference between the proposed ordinances and the voluntary pledges lies in their legal enforceability and scope.

**Voluntary Pledges:**
*   **Nature:** These are non-binding commitments that employers are asked to sign, such as the "Constitutional Pledge to California Workers’ Rights." Adherence is entirely at the employer's discretion, as evidenced by some employers declining to affirm support.
*   **Content:** They outline desired actions, like ensuring workers have access to private, protected areas and that immigration enforcement officers do not enter without a judicial warrant.
*   **Enforceability:** Since they are voluntary, there is no legal mechanism to compel an employer to follow these protocols if they choose not to sign or adhere to the pledge.

**Proposed Ordinances:**
*   **Nature:** These are proposed local laws (like a "fast food fair work ordinance") that, if passed, would be legally binding and enforceable. They would establish mandatory standards and protections.
*   **Content:** An ordinance would legally mandate specific actions, such as providing paid trainings for workers to learn about their rights during immigration enforcement actions. Unlike a pledge, which is a promise, an ordinance creates a legal obligation.
*   **Enforceability:** Once enacted, an ordinance would carry the force of law, meaning employers would be legally required to comply with its provisions. Non-compliance could lead to penalties or legal action, providing a robust mechanism to ensure employee safety protocols are followed, rather than relying on an employer's goodwill or voluntary commitment.

Title: ‘They singled out non-white, foreign-born workers’: the restaurants raided by Britain’s version of ICE 
Date: 2026-03-24

Diners were tucking into their upmarket Indian lunch when the Ice agents slid through the restaurant’s back fence. Clad in stab vests, the 11-strong unit blocked off every entrance before moving in on their target: Mandira’s Kitchen. This wasn’t a scene from California or Texas. It happened near Guildford, England, among the rolling Surrey Hills. Before the Home Office’s immigration compliance and enforcement (Ice) officers stormed the restaurant in September, they came up with a codeword in the event they were attacked with any weapons that might be at hand in a kitchen. What they found were customers eating biryani and samosas in a converted barn decorated with plants and a rickshaw bicycle hanging from the ceiling. When they reached the kitchen, they found five junior members of staff cooking. The officers demanded to see their passports. “They didn’t explain. They didn’t ask for permission,” says the restaurant’s owner, Mandira Moitra Sarkar. That 11 officers could burst into her business with no warrant and question staff is “astounding”, she says. Moitra Sarkar was on holiday in Tanzania when Ice arrived; she was notified by a frantic call from a member of staff. Moitra Sarkar made her name in the restaurant industry after being lauded by culinary giants including Nigella Lawson and Jamie Oliver. Her prepared dishes, which can be bought online for home delivery, were featured on a Channel 4 competition to find the country’s best ready meals for Aldi. But this wasn’t enough for the Home Office. Its crackdown on illegal working has left many business owners and employees reeling from its heavy-handed tactics. The immigration officers – from South Central Ice, which covers a patchwork of southern England including Portsmouth, Plymouth and Surrey – were accompanied by two officers from Surrey police, who walked around the restaurant among the customers, watching the scene unfold, and then blocked entry to the toilets. Once the Ice officers entered the kitchen, the members of staff were separated and questioned individually. One employee was asked whether his name was really Shamriaz (officers insisted it was Shabbir); another was quizzed on his wife and his child; one was asked about their studies after showing a university ID. Moitra Sarkar says the officers asked questions such as: “When was the last time you went to university? What grades do you get? Do you get your assignments done on time?” She believes it was “completely ad hoc”. After 37 gruelling minutes, having failed to find any wrongdoing, the Ice officers left the premises. To top it all off, Moitra Sarkar says, the Home Office vans left the restaurant car park without paying – non-customers are usually charged £2. The raid is one of more than 17,400 on businesses carried out since Labour came to power in July 2024 – a 77% increase on the year before and almost as many as in the entire previous parliament. The government says the drive has led to the highest number of arrests for illegal working since records began, but critics say the crackdown goes far beyond the party’s manifesto pledge of a “fair and properly managed immigration system”. While the UK’s Ice teams were set up in 2013 as part of Theresa May’s “hostile environment” policy, Labour has been using the increased number of raids to front much of the Home Office’s media output. In January, the Home Office set up a TikTok account, @SecureBordersUK, to show Ice officers raiding a market, a car wash and a nail bar. One video, which appears to show people struggling in open water during small-boat crossings, contains a stark message: “To the migrants who come to the UK illegally: you will face deportation or removal.” Most businesses raided by Ice don’t know why they were targeted. Anonymous tipoffs, which can be made online, appear to be behind many of the raids (including the one on Mandira’s Kitchen). Kevin Barker, a former Ice officer and the director of the paralegal firm Immigration Compliance Ltd, says that while raids are always “intelligence-led”, a tipoff can be enough to trigger one. There are steps between a tipoff being filed and a raid being carried out, Barker says. Surveillance is sometimes used, including “discreet drive-by surveillance”. Barker says Ice will also “see if there’s any other allegations or previous immigration raids to the business” and investigate if a name is given. But more often than not, he says, “there are no names mentioned – they just have a suspicion of illegal workers”. The nature of anonymity means a tipoff can relate to a personal or commercial grievance. Often, it’s “competitors within the local area” filling out the forms, he says. Moitra Sarkar holds a sponsor licence, which allows her to employ students or workers who might not normally have the right to work in the UK. “It was all very intimidating. Oh, you’re brown and you’re running a food business? Of course you’ve got illegal immigrants. It’s guilty until proven innocent,” she says. Racial profiling is a factor, says Seema Syeda, the advocacy and communications director at the Joint Council for the Welfare of Immigrants, which provides legal advice for those targeted by raids. She says the high number of arrests doesn’t paint the full picture. In 2025, Ice officers carried out 12,791 illegal-working raids and made 8,971 arrests. However, the Home Office’s figures show that only a quarter of those arrested (2,251) were detained and 12% (1,087) left the UK, either by force or voluntarily. Syeda says she has seen “many cases where people are raided, arrested and then released”. It is, she says, “very clear to us that this is a performative act”. *** When I arrive at Facing Heaven, the owner, Julian Denis, looks hesitant. I’m wearing all black and, for a moment, he worries Ice has returned. His business, a trendy vegan Chinese restaurant near Broadway Market in east London, was raided in September. It was Saturday night and the restaurant, decorated in bright pink and neon, was packed. About 10 officers made a beeline for the open-plan kitchen. They separated the staff before interrogating them individually, asking to see their IDs and payslips. Apart from speaking to the restaurant’s general manager, James Nolan, the officers didn’t speak to any white members of staff that night. They “singled out non-white, foreign-born workers for interrogation, scaring them with the possibility of being immediately jailed”, says Nolan. The lead officer told Nolan they were there off the back of an anonymous tipoff that specified “illegal Bengali workers”. After failing to find any illegal workers in the kitchen, having carried out their interrogations in plain sight of diners, the officers asked Nolan if there were any more members of staff on the premises. He said there weren’t. He was then asked the question again, replying with the same answer. Upon being asked a third time, Nolan got frustrated and said not “unless they’re on the fucking roof”. At this point, the officer “gave up trying to catch me out”, he says. The officer told Nolan that they were looking for “five to six illegal workers”. After about half an hour, with the dinner service halted, Ice left. Nolan asked an officer why they came during their busiest period, allowing customers to “look at this charade unfold”. The officer said they had to come at a “busy time when all staff were working”. Denis is from the US, where Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has instilled fear in communities of colour by rounding up and detaining people – many of them legal residents or even US citizens – using aggressive and violent tactics. This feeling has been intensified by the filmed killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti by federal agents. Denis describes the raid on his restaurant as “a wake-up call”. He says his employees, who are “legally employed and have the full right to work in the UK, should not have to come to work feeling fearful because of their country of origin”. Denis has a two-word message for the Home Office: “Fuck Ice.” Mandira’s Kitchen and Facing Heaven were raided under the Licensing Act 2003, which makes restaurants particularly vulnerable to surprise visits. It allows Ice officers to enter a business when it is carrying out a licensed activity, such as serving alcohol or selling hot food or drinks after 11pm, without a warrant. In 2025, 28% of all raids were carried out on restaurants, takeaways and cafes, a proportion significantly higher than for any other type of business. Barker claims that officers often go beyond the powers of the act, which allows them only to enter a business. Once inside, they treat it as though they have “carte blanche to speak to whoever they want to and search wherever they want to”. For example, Ice could enter a restaurant on the basis of concerns over someone without the right to work selling alcohol, but end up rounding up kitchen staff or delivery drivers, who “have no part in the sale of alcohol”. Ice was given greater power to enter businesses in 2017 after amendments to the Licensing Act were passed. The changes had some notable critics, including the MP for Holborn and St Pancras, Keir Starmer, who told the Commons in 2015 that they would give officers a “much lower threshold than the usual threshold for entering premises”. Barker says the amended act has proved “very powerful” for Ice: “Quite often, I would argue that their intentions have not been anything to do with the licensed activity. It’s just used to give them the power to enter.” In Surrey, Moitra Sarkar is still seeking answers. She sent freedom of information requests to the Home Office and Surrey police to find out why her business had been raided. The manager on shift at the time had overheard officers say they had received a tipoff, which has since been confirmed by documents she has obtained. These documents also reveal that the Home Office contacted the council to ask whether it had “licensing concerns” (it didn’t) and invited council officers to join the raid, which Moitra Sarkar believes was an attempt to beef up the legal justification. The “pre-visit report” lays out how Ice used the Licensing Act to authorise its raid without a warrant. It cites the cafe’s website, showing a menu including alcoholic drinks, as evidence that a licensed activity was taking place, which, combined with the tipoff, gave officers the power to enter. Officers are supposed to get “fully informed consent” from the business owner, which involves clearly saying why they are there and what they plan to do, as well as the potential civil or criminal penalties that could result from their visit. Businesses should also be told that they can refuse Ice officers entry and can withdraw their consent at any time. However, when Ice raided Mandira’s Kitchen, Moitra Sarkar says, the general manager asked the officer whether they could come back another day. The Surrey police officer told her: “No, that’s not how it works,” and pressed ahead anyway. When the Guardian asked about the raid, Surrey police said Ice and officers from its modern slavery and organised immigration crime unit carried out a joint visit “to complete a routine licensing check under section 179 of the Licensing Act 2003”. Fizza Qureshi, the CEO of the charity Migrants’ Rights Network (MRN), has spoken to a number of affected business owners, many of whom are from migrant backgrounds. She says many are “not fully informed of what their rights are” and have become resigned to raids being “part of their daily life”. The Home Office doesn’t record ethnicity data when carrying out raids or arrests (although it does record nationality), but research from MRN suggests they are carried out in areas that have large black and Asian populations. Qureshi has spoken to a car wash owner who says he is raided every three months or so, despite no illegal workers being found. The impact can be profound, she says, with raided businesses struggling to shake off the stigma. A Home Office spokesperson said: “All enforcement operations are intelligence-led. Race and ethnicity play no role in operational decisions. Illegal working undermines honest employers, undercuts local wages and fuels organised immigration crime.” “We hold our officers to the highest standards of professionalism and categorically refute any suggestion they acted improperly [or] were heavy‑handed,” the spokesperson added. Tanya Goldfarb, the head of business immigration at the law firm Bindman’s, says care homes, IT companies and hotels have also been targeted by Ice recently. Goldfarb says the raids “are frightening and intimidating” and that people may be handcuffed: “They are taken to detention centres. Sometimes they’re released and sometimes they’re not released. People often don’t know where their loved ones are. They don’t have a chance to make a phone call to anybody.” While Ice has far fewer powers than ICE in the US, this may not remain the case. Reform UK and the Conservatives have announced plans to create agencies modelled on ICE, should they win power. However, the increase in raids hasn’t come without opposition on the ground. Anti-raid groups are “springing up”, says Syeda. In January, a group in Lewisham, south London, alerted people to a car wash raid. When protesters told those being approached by Ice officers of their rights, no one was arrested. For Moitra Sarkar, the raid came during a particularly tough time. Days earlier, more than 100,000 people had marched through central London for “unite the kingdom”, a far-right rally at which racist conspiracy theories and anti-Muslim hate speech rang through Whitehall. Her daughter, who was born and raised in Surrey, was told not to go to work that week. “I’ve lived in Britain for 28 years and I’m the most optimistic person that there is,” says Moitra Sarkar. “But so many things happened that week.” Aside from the personal impact of the raid, Moitra Sarkar thinks it was a waste of officers’ time and taxpayers’ money. “We really need to call it out for what it is,” she says. “Completely ludicrous.” • Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in our letters section, please click here. • This article was amended on 24 March 2026. An earlier version referred to Ice officers being armed with stun guns during a raid; this was based on information from a witness account that was put to the Home Office prior to publication. However, the Home Office has since informed us that Ice officers do not possess such devices. An additional comment from the Home Office was added on 25 March 2026.

conversation.rag_input: That sounds incredibly aggressive for the restaurant staff. How did the shop owner's experience in the US compare to that, given the lack of a warrant in their case as well?
conversation.question: How did the interrogation and arrest tactics used on the shop owner in the US compare to the methods used during the UK restaurant raids, specifically regarding the use of physical force and the presence of warrants?
conversation.answer: Both scenarios involved highly aggressive, non-consensual interactions where legal oversight was noticeably absent. In the UK, the Home Office officers used the Licensing Act to justify entry without a warrant, relying on psychological intimidation and invasive personal questioning to target foreign-born workers. In contrast, the US incident involved the use of direct physical force, with officers reportedly kneeling on the shop owner’s back during the arrest. While the UK approach focused on systematic, status-based interrogation of employees, the US incident demonstrated a greater degree of immediate physical violence and physical restraint against the business owner, though both cases shared an underlying pattern of bypassing standard due process protections like warrants.
conversation.rag_answer:
The shop owner's experience with immigration officers, while also lacking a warrant, presented a different form of aggression compared to the restaurant raids, characterized by immediate physical violence and detention.

Here's a comparison:

**Similarities (Lack of Warrant and Profiling):**
*   **Lack of Warrant/Justification:** In both cases, officers proceeded without presenting a warrant or providing a clear reason for their actions. Juanita Avila, the shop owner, was arrested without a warrant or probable cause, a tactic later deemed "brutal and violent" and systematically violating federal law by a judge. Similarly, restaurant owners noted officers "didn’t explain. They didn’t ask for permission" for their raids.
*   **Targeting/Profiling:** Both incidents involved clear targeting. In the restaurant raids, officers explicitly "singled out non-white, foreign-born workers" and were looking for "illegal Bengali workers," leading to a sense of "guilty until proven innocent." Juanita's arrest was part of "Operation Black Rose," which targeted individuals without warrants, often based on skin color or industry, and she was accused of being "illegal" despite possessing a green card, with an agent dismissing her explanation by stating, "Because they lie all the time."

**Differences (Nature of Aggression and Interrogation):**
*   **Physicality and Immediate Force:** Juanita's arrest was marked by extreme physical aggression. She was tackled facedown on the pavement, pinned down by masked officers, pulled out of her van, kneeled on her back, handcuffed, and thrown into an ICE vehicle "like a log." She sustained injuries and suffered a panic attack. Her daughter was also threatened with arrest for "obstruction" for filming and had a bright light shined in her face to obscure her camera.
*   **Interrogation Style in Shop Owner's Case:** The "interrogation" was brief and confrontational. Officers immediately demanded to know where she was born, declared her under arrest, and accused her of being "illegal." There was no prolonged questioning about personal details, rather a quick dismissal of her legal status and an immediate, forceful detention.
*   **Interrogation Style in Restaurant Raids:** While intimidating (officers in stab vests, blocking entrances, halting service), the restaurant raids involved more prolonged and intrusive verbal questioning. Staff were separated and questioned individually, asked about personal details like university studies, family, and assignments. Officers also demanded to see specific documentation like passports, IDs, and payslips, and managers were repeatedly questioned in an attempt to "catch them out."

In essence, while both scenarios demonstrated aggressive and unwarranted tactics rooted in profiling, the shop owner's experience was characterized by immediate, brutal physical force and detention, whereas the restaurant raids focused more on an intimidating, intrusive, and racially targeted verbal interrogation process within the business premises.
